> - **Wet Chemical extinguishers** are like *cooking the bucket itself* (saponification — forming a soapy blanket on hot oil).

Understanding this analogy makes it instantly clear **why** each extinguisher is matched to a specific class of fire.

## 1.4 Classification of Fires (IS 15683 / NFPA 10 Standard)

Before selecting an extinguisher, one must identify the **fire class**. KTU follows the internationally harmonized classification:

| Fire Class | Fuel Type | Typical Examples |
|------------|-----------|------------------|
| **Class A** | Ordinary combustibles | Wood, paper, cloth, rubber, plastics |
| **Class B** | Flammable liquids & gases | Petrol, diesel, LPG, acetone, paints |
| **Class C** | Flammable gases (under pressure) | Methane, propane, hydrogen |
| **Class D** | Combustible metals | Magnesium, sodium, potassium, titanium |
| **Class E** *(or Class C in NFPA)* | Electrical (energized) | Live switchgear, motors, transformers |
| **Class F** *(or Class K in NFPA)* | Cooking oils & fats | Deep-fryer fires, vegetable/animal oils |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Indian Standard **IS 15683** uses Class E for *electrical fires*, whereas NFPA (USA) uses Class C. Always use the IS classification in KTU exams unless the question explicitly states NFPA.

## 1.5 Colour Coding of Fire Extinguishers (IS 15683)

In India, extinguishers are predominantly **all RED** (signal red, RAL 3000) for the body, with a colour-coded **band or label** indicating the extinguishing agent:

| Band Colour | Extinguisher Type |
|-------------|-------------------|
| Signal Red | Water (stored pressure) |
| Cream | Foam (AFFF) |
| Blue | Dry Powder (ABC) |
| Black | Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) |
| Yellow | Wet Chemical (Class F) |
| French Blue | Clean Agent (HCFC/HFC) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of Extinguishment

Every extinguisher must attack the fire through **one or more** of these mechanisms:

1. **Cooling** — Reducing the fuel temperature below its *fire point* (mainly water-based).
2. **Smothering** — Excluding oxygen from the fuel surface (foam, CO₂, dry powder).
3. **Fuel Removal** — Physically or chemically isolating the fuel (wet chemical saponification).
4. **Chain-Breaking (Inhibition)** — Interrupting the radical chain reaction in the flame zone (dry chemical powder, clean agents).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Most modern multi-purpose dry chemical extinguishers work via *two* simultaneous mechanisms — smothering (powder cloud) **and** chain-breaking (radical scavenging). This dual action is why ABC powder is the most versatile portable extinguisher.

## 2.2 Heat Removal Capacity Equation (Water-Based Extinguishers)

The cooling effectiveness of a water extinguisher can be expressed as the **heat absorption capacity**:

$$
Q_{abs} = m_{w} \cdot c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T + m_{w} \cdot L_{v}
$$

Where:
- $Q_{abs}$ = Total heat absorbed by water (kJ)
- $m_{w}$ = Mass of water discharged (kg)
- $c_{pw}$ = Specific heat capacity of water $\approx 4.186 \; \text{kJ/kg·°C}$
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature rise of water from ambient to $100\;°\text{C}$ (°C)
- $L_{v}$ = Latent heat of vaporization of water $\approx 2260 \; \text{kJ/kg}$

## 2.3 Discharge Time & Range Equations

$$
t_{d} = \frac{V_{cyl}}{Q_{rate}}
$$

$$
R_{eff} = k \cdot \sqrt{P_{gauge}}
$$

Where:
- $t_{d}$ = Total discharge time (s)
- $V_{cyl}$ = Internal cylinder volume (L)
- $Q_{rate}$ = Volumetric discharge rate (L/s)
- $R_{eff}$ = Effective throw range (m)
- $P_{gauge}$ = Gauge pressure of propellant gas (bar)
- $k$ = Empirical nozzle coefficient (typically $0.8 - 1.2$)

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbol | Units | Application |
|---|---------------------|--------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $Q_{abs} = m_{w} c_{pw} \Delta T + m_{w} L_{v}$ | Heat absorbed | kJ | Water extinguisher cooling capacity |
| 2 | $\text{Fire Point} < T_{fuel} < \text{AIT}$ | Extinguishment condition | °C | Determining cooling target |
| 3 | $t_{d} = V_{cyl} / Q_{rate}$ | Discharge duration | s | Sizing extinguisher charge |
| 4 | $R_{eff} = k\sqrt{P_{gauge}}$ | Throw range | m | Nozzle design |
| 5 | $\text{Critical} \;\; [O_2] < 12\%$ | Extinguishment threshold | \% vol | Inert gas / foam smothering |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{powder} = C_{d} A_{n} \sqrt{2 \rho_{b} \Delta P}$ | Powder discharge rate | kg/s | Dry chemical extinguisher |
| 7 | $\rho_{CO_2} \;(g) \approx 1.98 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ at $0\;°\text{C}$ | Gas density | kg/m³ | CO₂ storage calculations |
| 8 | $\text{ABC Rating: } N \cdot A \; B$ | Fire size capacity | — | IS 15683 / BIS rating |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** When a numerical problem appears, examiners expect students to **explicitly state the formula, substitute values with units, and report the final answer with units**. Always show $c_{pw} = 4.186 \; \text{kJ/kg·°C}$ rather than just using the symbol.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Industry

- **Refineries & Petrochemicals:** Foam monitors (AFFF) + Dry Powder for *Class B* bulk storage fires.
- **Data Centres & Server Rooms:** Clean Agent (FM-200 / Novec 1230) — leaves no residue, non-conductive.
- **Commercial Kitchens:** Wet Chemical (Class F) — saponification reaction forms a soap blanket.
- **Electrical Substations:** CO₂ — leaves no residue, non-conductive.
- **Aluminium/Magnesium Plants:** Special Class D powders (NaCl, Cu powder, graphite-based).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Classification & Working of Each Extinguisher Type

### 3.1.1 Water-Type Fire Extinguishers (Class A Only)

**Variants:**
- **Stored Pressure** — Nitrogen propellant at ~8–12 bar.
- **Pump-Type** — Manual pumping; no propellant gas.
- **Back-Pack (Soda-Acid)** — Sulphuric acid reacts with sodium bicarbonate to generate CO₂ in-situ.

**Soda-Acid Reaction (Derivation):**

$$
\text{NaHCO}_3 \; (aq) + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \; (aq) \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 \; (aq) + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \; (l) + 2\text{CO}_2 \; (g) \uparrow
$$

**Step-by-Step Evaluation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Molar masses:} \quad & M_{\text{NaHCO}_3} = 84 \; \text{g/mol} \\
& M_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} = 98 \; \text{g/mol} \\
& M_{\text{CO}_2} = 44 \; \text{g/mol} \\
\text{For } 2 \text{ mol NaHCO}_3: \quad & 2 \times 84 = 168 \; \text{g produces } 2 \times 44 = 88 \; \text{g CO}_2 \\
\text{Mass ratio:} \quad & \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{m_{\text{NaHCO}_3}} = \frac{88}{168} \approx 0.524
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** A 9-litre soda-acid extinguisher typically contains ~700 g NaHCO₃, generating ~366 g CO₂ — sufficient to expel the water charge.

**Mechanism:** Pure *cooling*. Water absorbs heat via $Q_{abs} = m_{w} c_{pw} \Delta T + m_{w} L_{v}$, lowering fuel below the *fire point*.

> [!WARNING]
> **DO NOT USE** water on Class B (oil floats on water, spreads fire), Class C (gas explosion risk), Class D (water reacts violently with burning metals: $2\text{Na} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{NaOH} + \text{H}_2 \uparrow$, producing explosive hydrogen), or Class E (electrocution hazard).

---

### 3.1.2 Foam-Type Fire Extinguishers (Class A & B)

**Agent:** Aqueous Film-Forming Foam (**AFFF**) — a mixture of fluoro-surfactants, foam stabilizers, and water.

**Working Mechanism:**
1. Foam floats on the surface of flammable liquids (lower density $\rho_{foam} \approx 0.1 - 0.3 \; \text{g/cm}^3$).
2. The aqueous film drains downward, **cooling** the fuel surface.
3. The foam blanket **smothers** by excluding oxygen (reduces local $[O_2]$ below 12 %).
4. Prevents re-ignition by blocking vapour release.

**Foam Expansion Ratio:**

$$
\text{ER} = \frac{V_{foam}}{V_{solution}}
$$

Where:
- $V_{foam}$ = Foam volume produced
- $V_{solution}$ = Original foam solution volume

For AFFF: $\text{ER} \approx 6 : 1$ to $10 : 1$ (Low/Medium Expansion). For high-expansion foams (used in aircraft hangars), $\text{ER} > 200 : 1$.

---

### 3.1.3 Dry Chemical Powder Extinguishers (ABC / BC)

**Most widely used extinguisher type globally.**

**Agent Composition:**

| Type | Active Agent | Mass % | Fire Class |
|------|--------------|--------|------------|
| ABC (Multi-purpose) | Monoammonium Phosphate $(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{HPO}_4$ | 40–75 % | A, B, C, E |
| BC (Regular) | Sodium Bicarbonate $\text{NaHCO}_3$ | 60–90 % | B, C, E |
| Specialized Class D | NaCl, Cu powder, Graphite | varies | D |

**Mechanism — Dual Action:**
1. **Smothering:** Powder cloud envelops flame, displaces oxygen.
2. **Chain-Breaking (Inhibition):** The phosphate radical $\text{PO} \cdot$ and the metal salt vapour catalytically recombine the flame-propagating free radicals ($\text{H} \cdot$, $\text{OH} \cdot$, $\text{O} \cdot$):

$$
\text{H} \cdot + \text{PO} \cdot \rightarrow \text{HPO} \; (\text{stable})
$$

$$
\text{OH} \cdot + \text{PO} \cdot \rightarrow \text{HPO}_2 \; (\text{stable})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **radical recombination** is why dry powder extinguishers are effective on flaming liquid fires even when the fuel temperature remains above its flash point.

**Pressure & Discharge:**
- Stored pressure: ~12–15 bar (N₂ propellant).
- Discharge time: 9–25 seconds depending on capacity.
- Effective range: 3–7 metres.

**BIS Rating Example:** "4A 55B" indicates extinguishing capacity for a 4 ft² wood-crib stack and 55 ft² heptane pool fire.

---

### 3.1.4 Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) Extinguishers (Class B & E)

**Storage:** Liquid CO₂ stored at ~58–70 bar in seamless steel cylinders at ~21 °C.

**Critical Properties of CO₂:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Critical Temperature:} \quad & T_c = 31.04 \; °\text{C} \\
\text{Critical Pressure:} \quad & P_c = 7.38 \; \text{MPa} \;(73.8 \; \text{bar}) \\
\text{Triple Point:} \quad & T_{tp} = -56.6 \; °\text{C}, \; P_{tp} = 5.18 \; \text{bar}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since ambient temperature is **below** $T_c$, CO₂ remains in liquid-gas equilibrium inside the cylinder. Upon discharge, expansion cools the gas and a portion forms **solid CO₂ "snow"** (dry ice) at $-78.5 \; °\text{C}$ — providing a brief secondary cooling effect.

**Mechanism:**
- **Primary:** Oxygen displacement. CO₂ at ~34 % concentration in air reduces $[O_2]$ below the extinguishment threshold (~12 %).
- **Secondary:** Minor cooling effect via dry ice formation.

**Discharge Equation:**

$$
\dot{m}_{CO_2} = C_{d} A_{n} \sqrt{\rho_{liq} \left( P_{cyl} - P_{atm} \right) \cdot 2}
$$

**Limitations:**
- **No residue** — excellent for electrical fires, electronics, and food processing.
- **Suffocation hazard** — must NOT be used in confined spaces.
- Cannot be used on **Class A** fires effectively (no cooling — smouldering reignition risk).

---

### 3.1.5 Wet Chemical Extinguishers (Class F / K)

**Agent:** Potassium acetate $\text{CH}_3\text{COOK}$ or potassium citrate $\text{K}_3\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}_7$ dissolved in water.

**Mechanism — Saponification:**

When the alkaline solution contacts burning cooking oil (typically at $T > 250 \; °\text{C}$), it triggers the **saponification reaction**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Triglyceride (oil)} + \text{KOH (from acetate)} \rightarrow \text{K-Potassium Soap} + \text{Glycerol} + \text{Steam}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-Step Explanation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{CH}_3\text{COOK} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{KOH} + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Triglyceride} + 3\text{KOH} \rightarrow 3\text{R-COOK} \; (\text{soap}) + \text{Glycerol} \\
\text{Result:} \quad & \text{A thick, foamy soap blanket forms on the oil surface, blocking } O_2 \text{ and cooling.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The soap blanket has a **boiling point above cooking-oil temperatures** and is stable for hours, preventing re-ignition even if the oil remains hot.

---

### 3.1.6 Clean Agent Extinguishers (Class B, C, E)

**Examples:** FM-200 (HFC-227ea), Novec 1230 (FK-5-1-12), Halotron I, Halon 1211 *(phased out)*.

**Mechanism:** Primarily **chain-breaking (chemical inhibition)** — release fluorinated radicals that catalytically recombine combustion radicals, similar to dry powder but **without solid residue**.

**Selection Criterion — Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP) & Global Warming Potential (GWP):**

$$
\text{ODP}_{Halon} = 1.0 \quad ; \quad \text{ODP}_{FM-200} = 0 \quad ; \quad \text{ODP}_{Novec \; 1230} = 0
$$

$$
\text{GWP}_{FM-200} \approx 3500 \quad ; \quad \text{GWP}_{Novec \; 1230} \approx 1
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** The **Montreal Protocol (1987)** phased out Halons due to ODP = 1.0. Modern clean agents are **ODP-zero** but **GWP-conscious** — Novec 1230 is preferred for green buildings.

---

### 3.1.7 Class D (Metal Fire) Extinguishers

**Agents:**
- **Sodium Chloride (NaCl):** For Na, K, Mg, Al fires — forms a crust isolating the metal.
- **Copper Powder:** For Li fires (NaCl reacts with Li).
- **Graphite-based dry powder:** For high-temperature metal fires.

**Mechanism:** Smothering + thermal shielding. The powder melts and forms an inert crust that:
1. Excludes atmospheric oxygen.
2. Absorbs heat via $Q_{abs} = m_{powder} \cdot c_{p,eff} \cdot \Delta T$.
3. Prevents molten metal splashing.

> [!WARNING]
> **NEVER** use water, foam, ABC powder, or CO₂ on a Class D fire. Burning magnesium reacts violently with water, producing hydrogen gas which then explodes.

---

## 3.2 Comprehensive Comparison Matrix (KTU-Favourite Table)

| Property | Water | Foam | Dry Powder (ABC) | CO₂ | Wet Chemical | Clean Agent |
|----------|-------|------|------------------|------|--------------|--------------|
| **Fire Class** | A | A, B | A, B, C, E | B, E | F (K) | B, C, E |
| **Mechanism** | Cooling | Smothering + Cooling | Smothering + Inhibition | Smothering | Saponification | Inhibition |
| **Working Pressure (bar)** | 8–12 | 8–10 | 12–15 | 58–70 | 12 | 25–40 |
| **Discharge Time (s)** | 30–60 | 30–50 | 9–25 | 8–15 | 60–120 | 10–30 |
| **Range (m)** | 6–10 | 4–7 | 3–6 | 1.5–3 | 3–5 | 3–5 |
| **Residue** | Water | Foam | Heavy powder | None | Sticky foam | None |
| **Reignition Risk** | Low | Low | Low | **High** | Very low | Moderate |
| **Electrical Safe?** | **No** | **No** | **Yes** | **Yes** | **No** | **Yes** |
| **Suffocation Risk** | None | None | None | **Yes** | None | Mild |
| **Cost (Relative)** | Low | Low–Medium | Medium | Medium | High | Very High |
| **BIS / IS Code** | IS 6234 | IS 15518 | IS 15683 | IS 15683 | IS 15683 | IS 15683 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Generic Fire Extinguisher

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Grabs Handle] --> B[Squeeze Lever or Pull Pin]
    B --> C{Stored Pressure System}
    C -->|Yes| D[Propellant Gas N2 / CO2]
    C -->|No| E[Manual Pump or Cartridge]
    D --> F[Pressurised Cylinder with Agent]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Discharge Hose and Nozzle]
    G --> H{Agent Type}
    H -->|Water| H1[Cools Fuel Below Fire Point]
    H -->|Foam| H2[Smothers by Blocking O2]
    H -->|Dry Powder| H3[Inhibits Chain Reaction]
    H -->|CO2| H4[Displaces Oxygen]
    H -->|Wet Chemical| H5[Saponification of Oil]
    H -->|Clean Agent| H6[Radical Recombination]
    H1 --> I[Extinguishment Achieved]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> I
    H6 --> I
```

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Selecting the Correct Extinguisher

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Identify Fire Class] --> Q1{Fuel Type}
    Q1 -->|Wood/Paper/Textile| P1[CLASS A]
    Q1 -->|Flammable Liquid| P2[CLASS B]
    Q1 -->|Flammable Gas| P3[CLASS C]
    Q1 -->|Combustible Metal| P4[CLASS D]
    Q1 -->|Live Electrical| P5[CLASS E]
    Q1 -->|Cooking Oil/Fat| P6[CLASS F]
    P1 --> R1[Water or ABC Powder]
    P2 --> R2[Foam, ABC Powder or CO2]
    P3 --> R3[ABC Dry Powder]
    P4 --> R4[Class D Specific Powder Only]
    P5 --> R5[CO2 or Clean Agent or ABC]
    P6 --> R6[Wet Chemical Extinguisher]
    R1 --> Z[Extinguish]
    R2 --> Z
    R3 --> Z
    R4 --> Z
    R5 --> Z
    R6 --> Z
```

## 4.3 Component Layout — Stored-Pressure Dry Chemical Extinguisher

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H1[Carrying Handle] --> H2[Discharge Lever]
    H2 --> H3[Pressure Gauge]
    H2 --> H4[Discharge Valve]
    H4 --> H5[Discharge Hose]
    H5 --> H6[Nozzle Assembly]
    H4 --> H7[SYPHON TUBE]
    H7 --> H8[DRY CHEMICAL POWDER CHARGE]
    H8 --> H9[NITROGEN PROPELLANT GAS at 12 to 15 bar]
    H9 --> H10[Seamless Steel Cylinder Body]
    H10 --> H11[ASME / IS Mark Certification Stamp]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Multi-Mechanism Extinguishment Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[FIRE TETRAHEDRON] --> SIDE1[FUEL]
    F --> SIDE2[OXYGEN]
    F --> SIDE3[HEAT]
    F --> SIDE4[CHAIN REACTION]
    WATER[Water] --> SIDE3
    FOAM[AFM Foam] --> SIDE2
    ABC[ABC Powder] --> SIDE2
    ABC --> SIDE4
    CO2[CO2] --> SIDE2
    WET[Wet Chemical] --> SIDE1
    CLEAN[Clean Agent] --> SIDE4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** List the **six classes of fire** as per **IS 15683**, with one example of fuel for each class.

**Model Answer:**

| Class | Fuel Type | Example |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| A | Ordinary combustibles | Wood, paper |
| B | Flammable liquids | Petrol, diesel |
| C | Flammable gases | LPG, methane |
| D | Combustible metals | Magnesium, sodium |
| E | Electrical (energized) | Live switchgear |
| F | Cooking oils/fats | Palm oil, vanaspati |

*(3 marks: 1 mark list of 4 classes, 1 mark remaining 2 classes, 1 mark correct examples)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Explain the **principle of chain-breaking (inhibition)** in fire extinguishment. Name one extinguishing agent that works on this principle.

**Model Answer:**

The **chain-breaking mechanism** interrupts the self-propagating free-radical reactions ($\text{H} \cdot$, $\text{OH} \cdot$, $\text{O} \cdot$) that sustain flame propagation. The extinguishing agent releases stable radicals (e.g., $\text{PO} \cdot$ from monoammonium phosphate) that catalytically recombine with flame radicals to form inert products, terminating the reaction chain.

**Example agents:** **Dry Chemical Powder (ABC)** — monoammonium phosphate; **Clean Agents** — HFC-227ea (FM-200); **Halon 1211** (now phased out).

*(3 marks: 2 marks explanation, 1 mark agent)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q (a)** Classify the different **types of fire extinguishers** based on the extinguishing agent used. For each type, state the fire class for which it is suitable and the working mechanism. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Fire extinguishers are classified based on the extinguishing agent stored in the cylinder. The selection depends on the fire class (IS 15683 classification).

**Classification & Mechanisms (6 × 1 mark each):**

1. **Water-Type Extinguisher (Stored Pressure / Pump-Type / Soda-Acid):**
   - *Suitable for:* Class A fires (wood, paper, textile).
   - *Mechanism:* **Cooling** — water absorbs heat ($Q_{abs} = m_w c_{pw} \Delta T + m_w L_v$), cooling fuel below its fire point.
   - *Limitation:* Cannot be used on Class B, C, D, or E fires.

2. **Foam Extinguisher (AFFF):**
   - *Suitable for:* Class A and Class B fires.
   - *Mechanism:* **Smothering + Cooling** — foam blanket reduces local $[O_2]$ below 12 %; aqueous film cools fuel surface.
   - *Limitation:* Not for live electrical or metal fires.

3. **Dry Chemical Powder (ABC / BC):**
   - *Suitable for:* Class A, B, C, E (multi-purpose ABC).
   - *Mechanism:* **Smothering + Chain-breaking** — powder cloud envelops flame; phosphate radicals ($\text{PO} \cdot$) inhibit combustion chain reaction.
   - *Limitation:* Leaves powder residue; can impair visibility.

4. **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) Extinguisher:**
   - *Suitable for:* Class B and Class E fires.
   - *Mechanism:* **Oxygen displacement** — CO₂ at 34 % concentration in air reduces $[O_2]$ below extinguishment threshold.
   - *Limitation:* Suffocation risk in confined spaces; high reignition risk on Class A fires.

5. **Wet Chemical Extinguisher:**
   - *Suitable for:* Class F (K) cooking-oil fires.
   - *Mechanism:* **Saponification** — alkaline potassium acetate reacts with burning oil to form a soap blanket, excluding oxygen and cooling.
   - *Limitation:* Specific to cooking oil fires.

6. **Clean Agent Extinguisher (FM-200 / Novec 1230):**
   - *Suitable for:* Class B, C, E (electronics, data centres).
   - *Mechanism:* **Chemical inhibition** — fluorinated radicals recombine with flame radicals.
   - *Limitation:* Very expensive; environmental concerns over GWP.

---

**Q (b)** With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the **construction and working of a stored-pressure dry chemical powder (ABC) extinguisher**. Mention the typical working pressure and discharge time. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (4 marks):**

A stored-pressure ABC extinguisher consists of the following components:

1. **Seamless Steel Cylinder** — body of the extinguisher, hydrostatically tested to ~25 bar.
2. **Dry Chemical Powder Charge** — typically 40–75 % monoammonium phosphate $(\text{NH}_4)_2\text{HPO}_4$, 1–4 kg in domestic units.
3. **Nitrogen Propellant Gas** — at **working pressure of 12–15 bar**, pre-charged in the cylinder.
4. **Pressure Gauge** — indicates readiness (needle in green zone = OK).
5. **Discharge Lever and Safety Pin** — pin prevents accidental discharge.
6. **Syphon Tube** — extends to the bottom of the cylinder to draw up powder.
7. **Discharge Hose and Nozzle Assembly** — directs the powder jet.

**Working (2 marks):**

- Pull the **safety pin** and squeeze the **discharge lever**.
- The pressurised N₂ gas pushes the dry powder up through the syphon tube.
- Powder is discharged via the nozzle as a high-velocity jet.
- Powder cloud **smothers** the flame (oxygen exclusion) and **chain-breaks** the combustion radicals.

**Specifications (1 mark):**
- *Working Pressure:* **12–15 bar** (N₂)
- *Discharge Time:* **9–25 seconds** (depending on capacity)
- *Effective Range:* 3–6 metres

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**Q (a)** Discuss in detail the **classification of fires** as per IS 15683. Why is **water not suitable** for Class B, C, D, and E fires? Explain with relevant chemistry where applicable. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification of Fires (IS 15683) — (3 marks):**

- **Class A:** Ordinary solid combustibles (wood, paper, cloth).
- **Class B:** Flammable liquids (petrol, diesel, paints).
- **Class C:** Flammable gases (LPG, methane, hydrogen).
- **Class D:** Combustible metals (sodium, magnesium, aluminium).
- **Class E:** Electrical fires (live switchgear, motors, transformers).
- **Class F:** Cooking oils and fats (kitchen fires).

**Why Water is NOT suitable for Class B, C, D, E — (4 marks):**

**Class B (Flammable Liquids):** Water is **denser** than most flammable liquids ($\rho_{water} = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$ vs. $\rho_{petrol} \approx 700 \; \text{kg/m}^3$). Water **sinks below** the fuel, and the fuel floats on top and continues to burn — often spreading the fire to a wider area.

**Class C (Flammable Gases):** Water cannot effectively cool a gas jet. High-pressure gas release can atomize water and create explosive steam clouds; no smothering effect.

**Class D (Combustible Metals):** Water reacts **violently** with burning metals, producing explosive hydrogen gas:

$$
2\text{Na} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{NaOH} + \text{H}_2 \uparrow \; (\text{explosive})
$$

$$
\text{Mg} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Mg}(\text{OH})_2 + \text{H}_2 \uparrow \; (\text{explosive})
$$

The liberated hydrogen gas can immediately ignite, causing a **steam explosion**.

**Class E (Electrical Fires):** Water is a good conductor of electricity. Discharging water on a **live** (energised) circuit causes **electric shock** to the operator and may create a short-circuit, spreading the fire.

---

**Q (b)** Explain the **saponification reaction** that occurs in a wet chemical extinguisher during a Class F fire. Why is wet chemical specifically chosen for cooking-oil fires instead of water or foam? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Wet Chemical Agent Composition (1 mark):**

The active agent is typically **potassium acetate** $\text{CH}_3\text{COOK}$ or potassium citrate dissolved in water, forming a mildly alkaline solution.

**Saponification Reaction (4 marks):**

Cooking oils are **triglycerides** — esters of glycerol and long-chain fatty acids. When the wet chemical solution contacts burning oil (typically at $T > 250 \; °\text{C}$), the following reaction occurs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 — Hydrolysis of acetate:} \quad & \text{CH}_3\text{COOK} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + \text{KOH} \\
\text{Step 2 — Saponification of triglyceride:} \quad & \text{Triglyceride} + 3\text{KOH} \rightarrow 3\text{R-COOK} + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3 \\
& (\text{Potassium soap}) \quad \quad \quad \quad (\text{Glycerol})
\end{aligned}
$$

The reaction is **endothermic**, absorbing heat from the burning oil. The resulting **potassium soap forms a thick, stable foam blanket** on the oil surface that:
1. Excludes atmospheric oxygen ($[O_2] < 12\%$).
2. Cools the fuel layer via continuous evaporation of entrained water.
3. Persists for hours because its boiling point exceeds that of cooking oil, preventing reignition.

**Why Not Water or Foam? (2 marks):**

- **Water** is dangerously ineffective on hot cooking oil: rapid vaporisation at $T > 100 \; °\text{C}$ causes **steam explosion** and **splashing** of burning oil, dramatically increasing fire spread and burn injuries. The latent heat of vaporisation $L_v = 2260 \; \text{kJ/kg}$ causes near-instantaneous flash-boiling.
- **Foam** (AFFF) breaks down at temperatures above ~200 °C and cannot form a stable blanket on oil above its auto-ignition temperature (~250 °C for most vegetable oils).
- **Wet chemical** is uniquely engineered for the chemistry of triglycerides and the high temperatures of cooking fires.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing NFPA vs IS 15683 fire classes** — IS uses Class E for electrical fires; NFPA uses Class C. Using the wrong terminology costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the BIS rating** (e.g., "4A 55B") when describing ABC extinguishers — examiners specifically test this in industrial safety modules.
> 3. **Not specifying the working pressure range** in construction questions — a vague "high pressure" answer loses 1 mark. Always state **12–15 bar for dry powder** and **58–70 bar for CO₂**.
> 4. **Mislabelling mechanisms** — students often write "cooling" for CO₂ or "smothering" for ABC powder only, missing the **dual mechanism** (smothering **+** chain-breaking).
> 5. **Skipping the chemistry** in saponification and Halon-substitution questions — KTU 2024 scheme emphasises *applied chemistry*, so formulae and reaction equations are mandatory.
> 6. **Spelling errors in IS code numbers** — write **IS 15683** correctly, not "IS-1568" or "IS:15683".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Fire Triangle → Fire Tetrahedron:** Modern understanding has four sides, not three. The *chain reaction* side is the unique fourth element.
- **Six Fire Classes:** A (solids), B (liquids), C (gases), D (metals), E (electrical), F (cooking oils).
- **Water works by cooling only** and is limited to Class A.
- **Foam** works by smothering + cooling — good for Class A and B but NOT electrical or metal fires.
- **Dry Chemical ABC Powder** is the **most versatile** portable extinguisher (A, B, C, E) — mechanism is **smothering + chain-breaking**.
- **CO₂** is residue-free — ideal for Class B and E, but reignition risk on Class A.
- **Wet Chemical** is **exclusively** for Class F cooking-oil fires — works via **saponification**.
- **Clean Agents** (FM-200, Novec 1230) replace phased-out Halons — ODP = 0, low GWP preferred.
- **Class D** requires **specialised dry powder** (NaCl for Na/K/Mg/Al; Cu powder for Li).
- **BIS Code IS 15683** governs portable extinguishers in India; colour-coded bands indicate agent type.
- **Soda-acid extinguisher reaction:** $2\text{NaHCO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 2\text{CO}_2 \uparrow$.
- **Saponification:** Triglyceride + KOH → Potassium Soap + Glycerol (endothermic; forms protective blanket).
- **Critical Pressure of CO₂:** $P_c = 7.38 \; \text{MPa}$ — liquid CO₂ exists below this critical temperature.
- **Extinguishment threshold:** $[O_2] < 12\%$ for most flammable fuels.
- **Always** identify the fire class *before* selecting an extinguisher — wrong choice can worsen the fire (e.g., water on burning oil = explosion).
- **PASS technique:** Pull → Aim → Squeeze → Sweep — standard operating procedure.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fire, Explosion and Toxic Gas Release
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET865 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fire, Explosion and Toxic Gas Release # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Fire — Formal KTU Definition > [!NOTE] > **Fire** is a rapid, self-sustaining **oxidation-reduction (redox) chemical reaction** that releases energy as **heat, light, and combustion products** (smoke, gases, soot). For sustained combustion to occur, three elements must be present simultaneously: **fuel, oxidizer (typically atmospheric oxygen), and an ignition source of sufficient energy.** In industrial safety engineering, fire is classified as one of the three principal **loss-of-containment (LOC)** hazards (the other two being **explosion** and **toxic gas release**). The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly treats these as the primary accident scenarios in any **process plant risk assessment**. **Conceptual Analogy — The Campfire Principle:** Think of a campfire like a three-legged stool. If you remove **any one leg**, the stool collapses. The three legs of a fire are: - **Fuel** (the wood — provides combustible vapor/gas), - **Oxygen** (the air — provides the oxidizer), - **Heat** (the match/flame — provides activation energy to break molecular bonds). Modern combustion science adds a **fourth leg** — the **chain reaction** of free radicals (H•, OH•, O•). This 4-element model is called the **Fire Tetrahedron**. ## 1.2 Explosion — Formal KTU Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Explosion** is the **sudden, violent, and instantaneous release of energy** accompanied by a **rapidly expanding gas front**, generating an **overpressure wave (shock wave)** that propagates through the surrounding medium at a velocity greater than the local speed of sound. Two physics regimes exist: 1. **Deflagration** — subsonic flame propagation ($v_{flame} < v_{sound}$, typically $< 300 \text{ m/s}$). Pressure rise is **moderate** (few psi). 2. **Detonation** — supersonic flame propagation with a leading shock front ($v_{flame} > v_{sound}$, 1500–3000 m/s). Pressure rise is **extreme** (10–100+ bar). **Conceptual Analogy — The Popcorn & Dynamite:** - **Deflagration** is like a popcorn kernel: energy is released rapidly but the surrounding air has time to move aside. A **propane cylinder** venting through a small hole and igniting is deflagration. - **Detonation** is like **dynamite**: the energy release is so violent that the air cannot move out of the way — it is **compressed into a shock wave** that can shatter concrete at hundreds of meters. ## 1.3 Toxic Gas Release — Formal KTU Definition > [!NOTE] > **Toxic Gas Release** is the **uncontrolled escape of a harmful chemical substance** (gas, vapor, aerosol, or dust) from its containment (pipe, vessel, tank), leading to atmospheric **dispersion** and potential **human exposure** above permissible health thresholds. The severity depends on: - **Concentration (C)** at the receptor — measured in **ppm (parts per million)** or **mg/m³**. - **Duration of exposure (t)** — typically 15 min, 30 min, or 1 hr. - **Toxicological potency** — quantified via **LC₅₀, TLV-TWA, IDLH**. **Conceptual Analogy — The Cigarette Smoke in a Closed Room:** When someone lights a cigarette in a sealed room, the smoke (toxic pollutant) does not stay localized — it **diffuses**, **drifts with air currents**, and its concentration drops with distance. A **toxic gas release** is mathematically identical: a continuous source emits a contaminant that the wind **advects** downstream while **turbulent eddies** dilute it laterally and vertically. The further you stand downwind, the safer you are (in most cases). ## 1.4 Industrial Context — Why These Hazards Matter > [!WARNING] > Per KTU Module 2 learning outcomes, you must be able to: > - **CO₂:** Identify flammability, explosivity, and toxicity parameters of hazardous materials. > - **CO₃:** Apply estimation techniques for **fireball radius**, **explosion overpressure**, and **toxic dispersion distance**. > > Failure to master these topics will cost **14-mark Part B questions** in ESE (End Semester Examination). --- ### Key Standard Metrics at a Glance | Metric | Symbol | Typical Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | Lower Flammable Limit | **LFL / LEL** | % vol in air | Below this, mixture is too lean to burn | | Upper Flammable Limit | **UFL / UEL** | % vol in air | Above this, mixture is too rich | | Flash Point | $T_{flash}$ | °C | Lowest temp at which vapor ignites with a flame | | Fire Point | $T_{fire}$ | °C | Temp at which sustained combustion occurs | | Auto-Ignition Temperature | **AIT** | °C | Self-ignition without external source | | Threshold Limit Value | **TLV-TWA** | ppm or mg/m³ | 8-hr time-weighted average exposure | | Immediately Dangerous | **IDLH** | ppm | Max concentration for 30 min without irreversible harm | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Fire Triangle vs. Fire Tetrahedron The classical **Fire Triangle** (Pillars) treats fire as needing three concurrent inputs. Modern combustion theory (supported by the **Hazardous Materials regulations** like **NFPA 30**, **OSHA 29 CFR 1910**) recognizes a fourth pillar: the **sustaining chain reaction** of free radicals. **Why a tetrahedron?** Because extinguishment can occur by disrupting any ONE of the four sides: - Remove **fuel** → fuel starvation (e.g., shut off gas valve). - Remove **oxidizer** → smothering (e.g., CO₂, foam, dry chemical). - Remove **heat** → cooling (e.g., water spray). - Break **chain reaction** → chemical inhibition (e.g., dry chemical powder interrupts H• + OH• radicals). ### 2.1.1 Flammability Characteristics (KTU Must-Know Parameters) For any **vapor-air mixture**, three critical temperatures define fire behavior: $$ T_{flash} < T_{fire} < T_{AIT} $$ **Where:** - **Flash Point ($T_{flash}$):** Minimum temperature at which a liquid gives off enough vapor to form an **ignitable mixture** near the liquid surface — but the flame does **not** continue after the ignition source is removed. - **Fire Point ($T_{fire}$):** Minimum temperature at which the vapor continues to burn for at least **5 seconds** after the ignition source is removed. - **Auto-Ignition Temperature ($T_{AIT}$):** Temperature at which the substance **self-ignites** without any external flame or spark. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Memory Trick:** "**F**lash needs a **F**lame. **F**ire **F**eeds itself. **A**IT = **A**ll by itself." ### 2.1.2 Flammability Limits - **LFL (Lower Flammable Limit):** Below this concentration, there is insufficient fuel to propagate flame. - **UFL (Upper Flammable Limit):** Above this concentration, there is insufficient oxygen to propagate flame. - The range **LFL ≤ C ≤ UFL** is the **flammability range**. **Le Chatelier's Rule** (for multi-component fuel mixtures): $$ LFL_{mix} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}} $$ Where $y_i$ is the mole fraction of component $i$ in the fuel mixture. ## 2.2 Explosion Theory — Types & Estimation Methods ### 2.2.1 Types of Industrial Explosions | Type | Description | Typical Yield | |---|---|---| | **Confined Vapor Cloud Explosion (VCE)** | Flammable vapor cloud ignited inside a building/vessel | Moderate | | **Unconfined VCE** | Vapor cloud drifts, finds ignition, burns in open air | Very High (e.g., 1947 Texas City) | | **BLEVE** | Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion | Massive | | **Dust Explosion** | Combustible dust dispersed in air | Very High (e.g., 2008 Imperial Sugar) | | **Mechanical Explosion** | Pressure vessel rupture | Variable | | **Detonation** | Supersonic shock-coupled flame | Catastrophic | ### 2.2.2 TNT Equivalency Method (Simplified Blast Damage Estimation) Used by **API RP 753**, **CCPS Guidelines**, and **KTU-validated problems**: $$ W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}} $$ **Where:** - $W_{TNT}$ = mass of TNT equivalent (kg) - $m$ = mass of flammable vapor (kg) - $\Delta H_c$ = heat of combustion of fuel (kJ/kg) - $E_{TNT}$ = heat of explosion of TNT = **4686 kJ/kg** - $\eta$ = **explosion efficiency factor** (typically $\eta = 0.01$ to $0.10$, common KTU value $\eta = 0.05$) ### 2.2.3 TNO Multi-Energy Method The **TNO** (Netherlands Organisation for Applied Scientific Research) method classifies explosions into **10 blast strength classes (1–10)** based on the degree of confinement. Class 10 represents a **fully confined detonation**, while Class 1 represents a weak deflagration. KTU problems typically use **Class 6–7** for partially confined VCEs. ### 2.2.4 Sachs Scaling Law (for side-on overpressure) $$ \Delta P_s = f\left(\bar{r}\right), \quad \bar{r} = \frac{r}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} $$ Where $\bar{r}$ is the **scaled distance** (m / kg$^{1/3}$). Tabulated Sachs values: | Scaled Distance $\bar{r}$ (m/kg$^{1/3}$) | Side-on Overpressure $\Delta P_s$ (kPa) | Damage | |---|---|---| | 0.3 | 1000 | Total destruction | | 1.0 | 280 | Heavy damage | | 3.0 | 90 | Severe structural damage | | 10.0 | 25 | Window breakage | | 30.0 | 7 | Minor window breakage | ## 2.3 Toxic Gas Release — Exposure Limits ### 2.3.1 Toxicological Reference Values | Parameter | Full Name | Exposure Duration | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | **TLV-TWA** | Threshold Limit Value – Time Weighted Average | 8 hours/day, 40 hr/week | Safe chronic exposure | | **TLV-STEL** | Short-Term Exposure Limit | 15 minutes | Safe short excursions | | **TLV-C** | Ceiling Limit | Instantaneous | Never exceed | | **IDLH** | Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health | 30 minutes | Max escape exposure | | **LC₅₀** | Lethal Concentration 50% | Acute (4 hr typical) | Kills 50% of exposed population | ### 2.3.2 Probit Equation (Y) for Toxicity & Blast Fatality The **Probit model** links the dose (concentration × time, or overpressure) to the probability of fatality. KTU syllabus explicitly includes this. **For toxic exposure:** $$ Y = a + b \cdot \ln(C^n \cdot t) $$ **For blast overpressure (lung rupture):** $$ Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \cdot \ln(P_s) $$ **Probability of fatality from Probit:** $$ P = 50 \left[1 + \frac{Y - 5}{\vert Y - 5 \vert} \cdot \text{erf}\left(\frac{\vert Y - 5 \vert}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] $$ A simplified KTU-acceptable approximation: $$ \% \text{ Fatality} = 50 \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] $$ ## 2.4 Complete KTU Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates **all** high-yield equations you MUST memorize for Module 2. Any KTU Part B question can be solved using these. | # | Formula | Description | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\displaystyle LFL_{mix} = \frac{1}{\sum y_i / LFL_i}$ | Le Chatelier's rule | Multi-fuel flammability | | 2 | $\displaystyle W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$ | TNT equivalency | Blast yield estimation | | 3 | $\displaystyle \bar{r} = \frac{r}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$ | Scaled distance | Overpressure mapping | | 4 | $\displaystyle \Delta P_s = f(\bar{r})$ | Sachs overpressure | Blast damage zones | | 5 | $\displaystyle \text{ME} = \frac{W_{TNT}}{m_{fuel}}$ | Mass Explosion Equivalency | TNT-equivalent % | | 6 | $\displaystyle C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \frac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | Gaussian plume dispersion | Continuous toxic release | | 7 | $\displaystyle Y = a + b \cdot \ln(C^n t)$ | Probit (toxic) | Dose-response | | 8 | $\displaystyle Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(P_s)$ | Probit (blast lung) | Overpressure fatality | | 9 | $\displaystyle R_{fireball} = k \cdot m^{1/3}$ | Fireball radius | BLEVE sizing | | 10 | $\displaystyle t_{duration} = 0.45 \cdot m^{1/6}$ | Fireball duration (s) | BLEVE timeline | ## 2.5 Real-World Industrial Utility The above equations are **not academic exercises** — they are used daily by: - **Process Safety Engineers** in oil & gas (Reliance, BPCL, Indian Oil). - **Risk Assessment Consultants** preparing **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** reports. - **On-site Emergency Planners** setting **evacuation radii** (e.g., a BLEVE of 1000 kg propane requires a **~290 m evacuation radius**). - **Government regulators** under the **Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989** (India). # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — TNT Equivalency for a Propane Leak **Problem:** A storage vessel containing **10,000 kg** of propane develops a leak. The cloud forms and ignites as an unconfined VCE. Estimate the **TNT equivalent mass** of the explosion. **Given:** - $m = 10{,}000$ kg of propane in the cloud - $\Delta H_c$ (propane) = **46,000 kJ/kg** - $\eta = 0.05$ (unconfined VCE, KTU standard assumption) - $E_{TNT} = 4686$ kJ/kg ### Step 1: Identify the governing equation The TNT equivalency formula is: $$ W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}} $$ This equation converts the **chemical energy** released by the fuel into an **equivalent mass of TNT**, which has a standardized blast energy output. The efficiency factor $\eta$ accounts for the fact that not all chemical energy converts to blast — much is lost as heat, light, and noise. ### Step 2: Substitute the numerical values $$ W_{TNT} = \frac{0.05 \times 10{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 46{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}}{4686 \text{ kJ/kg}} $$ ### Step 3: Compute the numerator The numerator equals the **effective energy released** by the propane explosion: $$ \eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c = 0.05 \times 10{,}000 \times 46{,}000 $$ Breaking it down: - First: $10{,}000 \times 46{,}000 = 4.6 \times 10^{8}$ kJ (total chemical energy) - Then: $0.05 \times 4.6 \times 10^{8} = 2.3 \times 10^{7}$ kJ (effective blast energy) So the effective energy released is: $$ \eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c = 2.3 \times 10^{7} \text{ kJ} $$ ### Step 4: Divide by TNT reference energy $$ W_{TNT} = \frac{2.3 \times 10^{7}}{4686} $$ Performing the division: $$ W_{TNT} = 4908.3 \text{ kg TNT equivalent} $$ ### Step 5: Result and Interpretation $$ \boxed{W_{TNT} \approx 4908 \text{ kg} \approx 4.91 \text{ tonnes of TNT}} $$ > **Interpretation:** The propane cloud, when ignited, releases energy equivalent to roughly **5 tonnes of TNT** — comparable to a small military demolition charge. This justifies the **500 m to 1000 m evacuation radius** mandated by the Indian National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA) guidelines for such VCE events. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Overpressure at a Receptor Point **Problem:** Using the result of Example 3.1, determine the **side-on overpressure** at a receptor located **$r = 300$ m** from the explosion center. **Step 1: Compute the scaled distance** The scaled distance (Hopkinson-Cranz scaling) is: $$ \bar{r} = \frac{r}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} $$ With $r = 300$ m and $W_{TNT} = 4908.3$ kg: $$ \bar{r} = \frac{300}{4908.3^{1/3}} $$ **Step 2: Compute $W_{TNT}^{1/3}$** $$ 4908.3^{1/3} = \sqrt[3]{4908.3} $$ Approximate by recognizing $17^3 = 4913$, hence: $$ W_{TNT}^{1/3} \approx 17.0 $$ **Step 3: Compute $\bar{r}$** $$ \bar{r} = \frac{300}{17.0} \approx 17.65 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3} $$ **Step 4: Look up Sachs' scaled overpressure** At $\bar{r} \approx 17.65$ m/kg$^{1/3}$, interpolation in the Sachs table gives approximately $\Delta P_s \approx 15$ kPa (about 2.2 psi). > **Interpretation:** This overpressure corresponds to **minor window breakage and potential injury from flying glass**. Any building within 300 m will likely sustain façade damage. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Probit Toxic Fatality Calculation **Problem:** A release of **chlorine** produces a ground-level concentration of **$C = 50$ ppm** for **$t = 30$ minutes** at a downwind residential location. Estimate the **percentage fatality**. **Given (for chlorine, from AIHA guidelines):** - $a = -19.83$ - $b = 3.51$ - $n = 1.0$ **Step 1: Apply the toxic Probit equation** $$ Y = a + b \cdot \ln(C^n \cdot t) $$ **Step 2: Compute $C^n \cdot t$** Since $n = 1.0$: $$ C^n \cdot t = 50^{1.0} \times 30 = 1500 \text{ ppm·min} $$ **Step 3: Compute $\ln(C^n t)$** $$ \ln(1500) = \ln(1.5 \times 10^3) = \ln(1.5) + 3 \ln(10) = 0.405 + 3 \times 2.303 = 0.405 + 6.908 = 7.313 $$ **Step 4: Substitute into Probit** $$ Y = -19.83 + 3.51 \times 7.313 $$ Computing the product: $$ 3.51 \times 7.313 = 25.67 $$ Then: $$ Y = -19.83 + 25.67 = 5.84 $$ **Step 5: Convert Probit to % fatality** Using the standard Probit-to-percent conversion: $$ \% \text{Fatality} = 50 \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] $$ Compute the argument: $$ \frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{5.84 - 5}{1.414} = \frac{0.84}{1.414} = 0.594 $$ From error function tables (or a calculator): $\text{erf}(0.594) \approx 0.595$ Therefore: $$ \% \text{Fatality} = 50 \times [1 + 0.595] = 50 \times 1.595 = 79.75\% $$ **Result:** $$ \boxed{\text{Approximately } 79.75\% \text{ fatality}} $$ > **Interpretation:** 50 ppm chlorine for 30 minutes is **catastrophic**. This justifies the **IDLH of 10 ppm** for chlorine — even small leaks demand full evacuation. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Multi-Component Flammability (Le Chatelier) **Problem:** A natural gas mixture contains **85% methane**, **10% ethane**, and **5% propane** (by volume). Compute the **LFL of the mixture**. **Given (LFL values from NFPA):** - LFL (methane) = **5.0%** - LFL (ethane) = **3.0%** - LFL (propane) = **2.1%** **Step 1: Apply Le Chatelier's equation** $$ LFL_{mix} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{y_{CH_4}}{LFL_{CH_4}} + \dfrac{y_{C_2H_6}}{LFL_{C_2H_6}} + \dfrac{y_{C_3H_8}}{LFL_{C_3H_8}}} $$ **Step 2: Substitute the values** $$ LFL_{mix} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{0.85}{5.0} + \dfrac{0.10}{3.0} + \dfrac{0.05}{2.1}} $$ **Step 3: Compute each term** $$ \frac{0.85}{5.0} = 0.170 $$ $$ \frac{0.10}{3.0} = 0.0333 $$ $$ \frac{0.05}{2.1} = 0.0238 $$ **Step 4: Sum and invert** $$ \text{Sum} = 0.170 + 0.0333 + 0.0238 = 0.2271 $$ $$ LFL_{mix} = \frac{1}{0.2271} = 4.40\% $$ **Result:** $$ \boxed{LFL_{mix} \approx 4.40\%} $$ > **Interpretation:** Mixing components with low individual LFLs (ethane, propane) pulls the **mixture LFL down** — making the mixture easier to ignite. This is why **LNG facilities** treat even small ethane contamination as a critical safety concern. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Method to Use? | Scenario | Recommended Method | Why? | |---|---|---| | Simple unconfined VCE, distance $< 1$ km | **TNT Equivalency** | Conservative, fast, exam-friendly | | Partially confined VCE in process unit | **TNO Multi-Energy** | Captures confinement effect | | Continuous gas stack release | **Gaussian Plume (Pasquill-Gifford)** | Steady-state dispersion | | Instantaneous puff release | **Gaussian Puff Model** | Transient dispersion | | Toxic dose-response | **Probit Equation** | Population fatality estimation | | Fireball from BLEVE | **Point-source radiation model** | Thermal hazard distance | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Fire Tetrahedron — Functional Architecture The diagram below maps the **Fire Tetrahedron** as a 4-element system with extinguishment pathways. This is the conceptual foundation for **selecting fire suppression media** in industrial plants. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fuel Source]:::fuel --> D[FIRE Event] B[Oxidizer Source]:::oxid --> D C[Ignition Energy]:::ign --> D E[Chain Reaction of Free Radicals]:::chain --> D A -. Remove fuel .-> F1[Fuel Starvation
Shut-off Valve] B -. Remove oxidizer .-> F2[Smothering
CO2 / Foam / Inert Gas] C -. Remove heat .-> F3[Cooling
Water Spray] E -. Break chain .-> F4[Inhibition
Dry Chemical Powder] D --> R1[Sustained Combustion] R1 --> R2[Heat Release] R1 --> R3[Light Emission] R1 --> R4[Smoke and Toxic Gases] classDef fuel fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF classDef oxid fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#006666,color:#000000 classDef ign fill:#FFE66D,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000000 classDef chain fill:#95E1D3,stroke:#2F4F4F,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.2 Explosion Damage Zones — Block Architecture The diagram below shows the **Sachs-scaled overpressure damage** flow from explosion center to receptor zones. Each shell represents a **specific damage threshold** and the corresponding casualties. ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC[Explosion Center
W TNT Equivalent] --> Z1[Zone 1
r less than 3 m/kg13
Total Destruction] Z1 --> Z2[Zone 2
r between 3 to 10
Heavy Structural Damage] Z2 --> Z3[Zone 3
r between 10 to 30
Window Breakage] Z3 --> Z4[Zone 4
r greater than 30
Minor Glass Crack] Z1 --> V1[Delta P greater than 280 kPa
100 percent Fatality] Z2 --> V2[Delta P between 90 to 280 kPa
Eardrum Rupture and Lung Damage] Z3 --> V3[Delta P between 25 to 90 kPa
Injury from Flying Glass] Z4 --> V4[Delta P less than 25 kPa
Hearing Discomfort Only] classDef srcNode fill:#FF4500,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF classDef zone1Node fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF classDef zone2Node fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF classDef zone3Node fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 classDef zone4Node fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 classDef validNode fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF class SRC srcNode class Z1 zone1Node class Z2 zone2Node class Z3 zone3Node class Z4 zone4Node class V1,V2,V3,V4 validNode ``` ## 4.3 Toxic Release — Dispersion and Response Architecture The diagram below models a **continuous toxic release** as a system of source-atmosphere-receptor interactions, showing how wind speed, stability class, and distance modify concentration. ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Toxic Source
Leak from Vessel or Pipe] --> D[Atmospheric Dispersion] D --> W[Wind Advection
Speed u m per s] D --> ST[Atmospheric Stability
Pasquill Class A to F] D --> TU[Turbulent Diffusion
sigma y and sigma z] W --> CON[Concentration at Receptor C x y z] ST --> CON TU --> CON CON --> E1{Compare C to TLV TWA} CON --> E2{Compare C to TLV STEL} CON --> E3{Compare C to IDLH} CON --> E4{Compare C times t to Probit Dose} E1 -->|C greater than TLV TWA| AC1[Chronic Health Risk
Long Term Exposure] E2 -->|C greater than TLV STEL| AC2[Acute Irritation Risk
Short Term] E3 -->|C greater than IDLH| AC3[Immediate Evacuation
Life Threatening] E4 -->|Dose greater than LC50| AC4[Fatality Estimation
Use Probit Equation] AC1 --> MIT[Risk Mitigation Measures] AC2 --> MIT AC3 --> MIT AC4 --> MIT MIT --> M1[Reduce Source Strength] MIT --> M2[Increase Ventilation] MIT --> M3[Use PPE] MIT --> M4[Evacuate Downwind Population] classDef sourceNode fill:#8B0000,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF classDef dispNode fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF classDef concNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 classDef effNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF classDef mitNode fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF class S sourceNode class D,W,ST,TU dispNode class CON concNode class E1,E2,E3,E4,AC1,AC2,AC3,AC4 effNode class MIT,M1,M2,M3,M4 mitNode ``` ## 4.4 Flammability Parameters Hierarchy The diagram below shows the **temperature hierarchy** of flammability parameters, with ignition energy and concentration overlap concepts. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Liquid Hydrocarbon in Storage] --> B[Increase Temperature] B --> C{Compare to Flash Point} C -->|T less than T flash| F1[No Flammable Vapor
Safe to Handle Openly] C -->|T equals T flash| F2[Flammable Vapor Forms
Ignitable with Flame Only] C -->|T greater than T fire| F3[Sustained Combustion
Vapor Burns Continuously] F2 --> D[Concentration in Vapor Cloud] F3 --> D D --> E{Compare to LFL and UFL} E -->|C less than LFL| G1[Too Lean
No Flame Propagation] E -->|LFL less than C less than UFL| G2[Flammable Range
Deflagration Possible] E -->|C greater than UFL| G3[Too Rich
No Flame Propagation] G2 --> H[Ignition Source Present] H --> I{Auto Ignition Check} I -->|T greater than AIT| J1[Self Ignition
No Spark Needed] I -->|T less than AIT| J2[Requires Spark or Flame] classDef liqNode fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF classDef tempNode fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 classDef flashNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 classDef concNode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 classDef flameNode fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF classDef ignNode fill:#8B0000,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF class A,B liqNode class C tempNode class F1,F2,F3 flashNode class D,E concNode class G1,G2,G3 flameNode class H,I,J1,J2 ignNode ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define the following terms with industrial examples:** (a) Lower Flammable Limit (LFL) (b) Flash Point (c) IDLH **Model Answer:** **(a) Lower Flammable Limit (LFL):** The **LFL** is the *minimum concentration of a flammable vapor in air* below which propagation of a flame does **not** occur on contact with a source of ignition. Below the LFL, the mixture is **too lean** to sustain combustion. **Example:** LFL of methane = **5.0%** by volume in air. Below 5%, a methane-air mixture cannot burn. **(b) Flash Point:** The **Flash Point** is the *lowest temperature* at which a liquid gives off sufficient vapor to form an **ignitable mixture** with air in a test vessel, but the flame does **not** propagate after the ignition source is removed. **Example:** Flash point of diesel = **52–96°C**, gasoline = **−43°C** (hence gasoline is highly hazardous at ambient temperatures). **(c) IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health):** The **IDLH** value represents the *maximum concentration* of an airborne contaminant to which an **unprotected worker** can be exposed for **30 minutes** without experiencing **irreversible health effects** or escape-impairing symptoms. **Example:** IDLH for chlorine = **10 ppm** (NIOSH); IDLH for ammonia = **300 ppm**. --- ### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Differentiate between Deflagration and Detonation with relevant examples.** **Model Answer:** | Property | **Deflagration** | **Detonation** | |---|---|---| | Flame speed | **Subsonic** (< 300 m/s) | **Supersonic** (1500–3000 m/s) | | Pressure rise | **Gradual** (0.5–5 bar) | **Near-instantaneous** (10–100 bar) | | Wave structure | **Pressure wave moves ahead of flame** | **Shock wave coupled with flame front** | | Damage pattern | *Pushing* damage (domed) | *Cratering* and *shattering* damage | | Energy dissipation | Slow, mostly heat | Fast, blast-dominant | | Example | **Propane gas stove flame** igniting after small leak; fireplace combustion | **TNT explosion**; detonation of acetylene at high pressure | > **Key Insight:** In deflagration, the **flame front lags behind** the leading pressure pulse. In detonation, the **flame front is driven by** the leading shock wave — making it far more destructive per unit mass. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Module 2 problems, students **lose marks** for the following mistakes: > - **Forgetting units** in TNT mass calculations (always write **kg TNT equivalent**). > - **Using incorrect efficiency factor $\eta$** — for unconfined VCE, use $\eta = 0.02$ to $0.05$; for confined, use $\eta = 0.1$ to $0.3$. > - **Applying Gaussian plume for instantaneous release** — the plume model is for **steady-state** continuous sources. For puffs, use the **puff model**. > - **Confusing LFL with LEL** — they are mathematically equivalent but LEL is the European term (Lower Explosive Limit). > - **Forgetting the $\ln$ term** in Probit calculations. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** **(a)** Explain the **Fire Triangle** and **Fire Tetrahedron** with neat diagrams. Describe how the four extinguishment principles relate to industrial fire suppression systems. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A storage tank containing **8000 kg of cyclohexane** develops a vapor cloud that ignites as an **unconfined VCE**. Estimate: - (i) The **TNT equivalent mass** of the explosion. - (ii) The **side-on overpressure** at a distance of **200 m** from the blast center. - (iii) The **% fatality** due to lung rupture at this location. Given: $\Delta H_c$ (cyclohexane) = **43,900 kJ/kg**, $E_{TNT}$ = **4686 kJ/kg**, $\eta = 0.04$. Use the Sachs table for overpressure and the lung-rupture Probit equation. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Fire Triangle vs. Fire Tetrahedron (7 marks):** The **Fire Triangle** consists of three elements that must coexist for fire: 1. **Fuel** (combustible material — solid, liquid, or gas) 2. **Oxidizer** (typically atmospheric oxygen, ≥ 16% concentration) 3. **Heat** (ignition source with sufficient activation energy) The **Fire Tetrahedron** adds a **fourth element — the chain reaction of free radicals** (H•, OH•, O•, etc.). Without continuous chain branching, the fire self-extinguishes. **Industrial suppression methods (mapped to the 4 sides):** - **Remove Fuel** → Shut-off valves on gas lines, drain burning liquid, stop process feed. - **Remove Oxidizer** → Foam blanket, CO₂ flooding, inert gas purging (e.g., nitrogen in storage tanks). - **Remove Heat** → Water spray cooling, deluge systems. - **Break Chain Reaction** → Dry chemical powder (monoammonium phosphate), Halon replacements (FM-200). > **Award 1 mark** for each correct extinguishment method mapped to its tetrahedron side. **(b) TNT Equivalent and Overpressure Calculation (7 marks):** **(i) TNT Equivalent Mass:** $$ W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}} $$ $$ W_{TNT} = \frac{0.04 \times 8000 \times 43{,}900}{4686} $$ Compute the numerator: $$ 0.04 \times 8000 = 320 \text{ kg} $$ $$ 320 \times 43{,}900 = 1.405 \times 10^{7} \text{ kJ} $$ Divide by TNT energy: $$ W_{TNT} = \frac{1.405 \times 10^{7}}{4686} = 2998 \text{ kg TNT} $$ **Result:** $\boxed{W_{TNT} \approx 3000 \text{ kg TNT equivalent}}$ — **(2 marks)** **(ii) Side-on Overpressure at 200 m:** Scaled distance: $$ \bar{r} = \frac{r}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} = \frac{200}{2998^{1/3}} = \frac{200}{14.42} \approx 13.87 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3} $$ From Sachs table (interpolation): - At $\bar{r} = 10$ m/kg$^{1/3}$, $\Delta P_s \approx 25$ kPa - At $\bar{r} = 30$ m/kg$^{1/3}$, $\Delta P_s \approx 7$ kPa Linear interpolation at $\bar{r} = 13.87$ m/kg$^{1/3}$: $$ \Delta P_s \approx 25 - \left(\frac{13.87 - 10}{30 - 10}\right) \times (25 - 7) = 25 - \frac{3.87}{20} \times 18 = 25 - 3.48 = 21.5 \text{ kPa} $$ **Result:** $\boxed{\Delta P_s \approx 21.5 \text{ kPa (≈ 3.1 psi)}}$ — **(2 marks)** > **Interpretation:** At 3.1 psi, the damage level corresponds to **window breakage and injury from flying glass**. **(iii) Fatality % due to Lung Rupture:** Lung-rupture Probit equation: $$ Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \cdot \ln(\Delta P_s) $$ Convert $\Delta P_s$ from kPa to Pa: $21.5 \text{ kPa} = 21{,}500 \text{ Pa}$ $$ \ln(21{,}500) = \ln(2.15 \times 10^4) = \ln(2.15) + 4 \ln(10) = 0.765 + 9.210 = 9.975 $$ $$ Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \times 9.975 = -77.1 + 68.93 = -8.17 $$ Compute % fatality: $$ \% \text{Fatality} = 50 \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] $$ $$ \frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{-8.17 - 5}{1.414} = \frac{-13.17}{1.414} = -9.31 $$ Since $\text{erf}(-9.31) \approx -1.0$: $$ \% \text{Fatality} = 50 \times [1 + (-1.0)] = 0\% $$ **Result:** $\boxed{0\% \text{ fatality}}$ at 200 m — the overpressure is too low to cause lung rupture, although **non-fatal injuries** are still expected. — **(3 marks)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **(a)** What is the **Probit equation** for toxic gas exposure? Derive the relationship between the Probit variable **Y** and the **percentage fatality**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An accidental release of **sulfur dioxide (SO₂)** produces a ground-level concentration of **$C = 30$ ppm** for a duration of **$t = 45$ minutes** at a downwind residential colony. Using the Probit equation for SO₂ with $a = -15.67$, $b = 2.10$, $n = 1.0$: - (i) Compute the **Probit value Y**. - (ii) Determine the **% fatality**. - (iii) Comment on whether evacuation should be ordered, given that **TLV-STEL for SO₂ is 5 ppm**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Probit Equation Derivation (7 marks):** The **Probit (Probability Unit)** model expresses the relationship between the **dose received by a receptor** and the **probability of a specific biological response** (injury or fatality). **General Form:** $$ Y = a + b \cdot \ln(D) $$ Where $D = C^n \cdot t$ is the **toxic dose** (concentration raised to power $n$, multiplied by exposure time). **Probit-to-Fatality Conversion:** The Probit variable **Y** is normally distributed with mean **5** and standard deviation **1**: $$ P = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y - 5} \exp\left(-\frac{u^2}{2}\right) du $$ Equivalently, using the **error function (erf)**: $$ P = 50 \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] $$ The **% fatality** is $P \times 100$. **Key points (1 mark each):** - Y = 5 corresponds to **50% fatality** (median lethal dose). - Y = 4 → ~16% fatality; Y = 5 → 50%; Y = 6 → 84%; Y = 7 → 97.7%. - For **Y > 5**, fatality increases sharply. - Constants $a$, $b$, $n$ are **substance-specific** (tabulated in AIHA, TNO Green Book). **(b) SO₂ Toxic Release Calculation (7 marks):** **(i) Probit Y:** $$ Y = a + b \cdot \ln(C^n \cdot t) = -15.67 + 2.10 \cdot \ln(30^{1.0} \times 45) $$ Compute: $$ 30 \times 45 = 1350 \text{ ppm·min} $$ $$ \ln(1350) = \ln(1.35 \times 10^3) = 0.300 + 3 \times 2.303 = 0.300 + 6.908 = 7.208 $$ $$ Y = -15.67 + 2.10 \times 7.208 = -15.67 + 15.137 = -0.533 $$ **Result:** $\boxed{Y \approx -0.53}$ — **(2 marks)** **(ii) % Fatality:** $$ \frac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{-0.533 - 5}{1.414} = \frac{-5.533}{1.414} = -3.913 $$ From error function tables: $$ \text{erf}(-3.913) \approx -1.000 $$ Therefore: $$ \% \text{Fatality} = 50 \times [1 + (-1.000)] \approx 0\% $$ **Result:** $\boxed{\% \text{Fatality} \approx 0\%}$ — **(2 marks)** **(iii) Evacuation Decision:** Although the **fatality probability is near zero**, the **concentration (30 ppm) is 6× the TLV-STEL (5 ppm)**. This means residents will experience: - **Severe respiratory irritation** - **Bronchoconstriction** (especially in asthmatics) - **Possible long-term pulmonary effects** with prolonged exposure > **Recommendation:** $\boxed{\text{IMMEDIATE EVACUATION IS JUSTIFIED.}}$ The acute health risk is severe, even though the lethal dose threshold is not reached within 45 min. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Fire, Explosion & Toxic Gas Release** ### Fire Fundamentals - Fire Triangle: **Fuel + Oxygen + Heat**. - Fire Tetrahedron: adds **Chain Reaction** (free radicals H•, OH•, O•). - **LFL** < **UFL**; flammability range is between them. - $T_{flash} < T_{fire} < T_{AIT}$. - **Le Chatelier's Rule** for multi-component LFL: $\displaystyle LFL_{mix} = \frac{1}{\sum y_i / LFL_i}$. ### Explosion Science - **Deflagration** = subsonic flame; **Detonation** = supersonic with shock front. - **TNT Equivalency:** $\displaystyle W_{TNT} = \frac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{4686 \text{ kJ/kg}}$ - **Sachs Scaling:** $\displaystyle \bar{r} = \frac{r}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$ → $\Delta P_s = f(\bar{r})$ from table. - Typical overpressure damage: - 0.5 psi → window breakage - 5 psi → eardrum rupture - 15 psi → lung damage - 65 psi → 99% fatality - **BLEVE** is a Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion (e.g., LPG tank rupture in fire). ### Toxic Release - **TLV-TWA** = 8-hr safe exposure. - **TLV-STEL** = 15-min safe exposure. - **IDLH** = 30-min escape exposure without irreversible harm. - **LC₅₀** = concentration lethal to 50% of population. - **Probit Y** = $a + b \ln(C^n t)$. - **% Fatality** = $50 [1 + \text{erf}((Y-5)/\sqrt{2})]$. ### Gaussian Dispersion - Continuous source concentration: $\displaystyle C = \frac{Q}{2\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \frac{z^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$. - $\sigma_y$ and $\sigma_z$ depend on **Pasquill stability class (A–F)** and **downwind distance**. - Classes A, B, C = unstable; D = neutral; E, F = stable. ### KTU Exam Strategy - Always show **units** in every calculation. - Mention $\eta = 0.05$ explicitly for unconfined VCE. - For TNT problems, convert kg → m³ vapor using fuel density when needed. - In Probit, always state the **substance-specific constants** ($a, b, n$) — examiners award 1 mark just for this. - Sketch the **Fire Tetrahedron** in any fire question (1-mark freebie).

topics.title: Structure of Hazard Identification and Risk Assessment
Fire and Explosion Hazard Rating of Process Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPET865 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fire and Explosion Hazard Rating of Process Plants # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Fire and Explosion Hazard Rating? **Fire and Explosion Hazard Rating** of process plants is a systematic, quantitative engineering methodology used to evaluate the relative magnitude of fire, explosion, and reactive chemical hazards associated with process units handling flammable, combustible, or reactive materials. It translates complex process information into a single numerical index that helps engineers, safety professionals, and management prioritize hazard control, allocate loss-prevention resources, and make informed decisions regarding plant layout, insurance, and emergency planning. > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):** > *Hazard Rating is a numerical score derived from material properties and process conditions that classifies the severity of potential fire and explosion incidents in a chemical/process plant. It provides a structured, reproducible framework for ranking process units based on the realistic loss potential if a worst-case incident occurs.* The two most widely used industrial rating systems in the world are: 1. **Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)** — developed by Dow Chemical Company (USA) 2. **Mond Index** — developed by Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI), UK These are semi-quantitative indices that balance engineering rigor with practical usability. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hazard Thermometer" Imagine a process plant as a human body exposed to a fever. A doctor doesn't just say "the patient is sick"; instead, they measure **temperature, blood pressure, heart rate** to assign a numerical severity. Similarly, a safety engineer cannot simply say "this plant is dangerous." The Hazard Rating acts like a **clinical thermometer for the plant**: - The **Material Factor (MF)** is the *baseline body temperature* — it tells you how inherently reactive the chemical is. - The **Process Hazards** are the *symptoms* — pressure, temperature, quantity, and operation mode. - The **Final F&EI Score** is the *consolidated diagnosis* — a single number that ranks danger. Just as a fever above 104°F is critical, an **F&EI above 128** is classified as "Severe," demanding aggressive loss-control measures. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Hazard Rating Matters in Industry:** > - **Insurance Premiums:** Insurers use these indices to calculate premiums for property and business interruption coverage. > - **Plant Siting & Layout:** Determines safe separation distances between process units. > - **Regulatory Compliance:** Aligns with OSHA PSM, EPA RMP, and Indian Factories Act requirements. > - **Capital Investment Justification:** Justifies spending on safety instrumentation, fire protection, and blast-resistant design. > - **Inherent Safety Design:** Identifies which units need process modification for inherent risk reduction. ## 1.3 The Dow Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI) — Primary Focus The **Dow F&EI** is the most established hazard rating index globally. It was first published by the Dow Chemical Company in 1964 and has undergone multiple revisions (the most cited is the **4th Edition, 1993** by Dow, though updates exist). The mathematical foundation of the Dow F&EI is: $$F\&EI = F_3 \times MF$$ Where: - $MF$ = Material Factor (inherent chemical energy) - $F_3$ = Process Unit Hazard Factor (a multiplier derived from General and Special Process Hazards) The index quantifies the **"radius of exposure"** and **"value of the area at risk"** — the financial and spatial extent of damage if an incident occurs in the worst credible scenario. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The Dow F&EI does *not* predict the probability of an incident. It estimates the **magnitude of consequences** assuming an incident occurs. This is a critical distinction students must understand for KTU exams. ## 1.4 Categories of Fire and Explosion Hazards Hazard rating evaluates four primary hazard phenomena: | Hazard Type | Description | Typical Consequence | |-------------|-------------|---------------------| | **Fire Hazard** | Pool fires, jet fires, flash fires from flammable liquids/gases | Thermal radiation, structural damage | | **Explosion Hazard** | VCE (Vapor Cloud Explosion), confined explosions, BLEVEs | Blast overpressure, fragmentation | | **Reactive Chemical Hazard** | Uncontrolled reactions, polymerization, decomposition | Runaway reactions, toxic releases | | **Toxic Release Hazard** | Often co-evaluated; covered separately in CEI/Dow CEI | Acute and chronic health impact | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Dow F&EI Methodology — Step-by-Step Logic The Dow methodology follows a strict 7-step procedure. KTU examiners frequently test this sequence. ### Step 1: Identify the Process Unit A **process unit** is any piece of equipment (reactor, distillation column, storage tank, heat exchanger) that operates independently and can be logically separated from other units. The unit with the **largest F&EI** typically governs the area's overall rating. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examination Tip:** A KTU favorite question is *"Define a process unit for the purpose of Dow F&EI calculation."* Memorize that it is a logical grouping of equipment, not necessarily a single vessel. ### Step 2: Determine the Material Factor (MF) The **Material Factor (MF)** is a dimensionless number (typically ranging from **1 to 40**) representing the intrinsic energy release potential of the most hazardous substance handled in the unit. Sources for determining MF: - **NFPA 704 Diamond** (Hazard rating — bottom-left blue quadrant for health, top yellow for reactivity, bottom-right red for flammability) - **NFPA 325M** (Fire Hazard Properties of Flammable Liquids, Gases, and Volatile Solids) - **DIPPR Database** (Design Institute for Physical Property Data) - **CHETAH Program** (ASTM) for reactive chemicals **Material Factor Categorization:** | NFPA Reactivity Rating (N) | NFPA Flammability Rating (F) | Typical MF Range | |---------------------------|------------------------------|------------------| | 0 (Stable) | 0–1 (Will not burn) | 1–4 (e.g., water, sand) | | 1 (Mild) | 1–2 | 4–10 (e.g., diesel) | | 2 (Moderate) | 3 (Combustible liquid) | 10–16 (e.g., kerosene) | | 3 (Severe) | 3–4 (Flammable) | 16–24 (e.g., acetone, gasoline) | | 4 (Extreme) | 4 (Very flammable gas) | 24–40 (e.g., hydrogen, acetylene, ethylene oxide) | For **unstable or reactive chemicals**, MF can exceed 40. A commonly cited example: **acrylonitrile (MF = 24)**, **vinyl chloride (MF = 24)**, **hydrogen (MF = 21)**, **ether (MF = 40)**. ### Step 3: Evaluate General Process Hazards (GPH) **General Process Hazards (GPH)** are six factors that apply to *any* process unit and contribute penalties of **0 to ~6 points** each. They represent routine operational risks. The six GPH factors are: 1. **A. Exothermic Chemical Reactions** (penalty 0 to 1.00) - 0 = No reaction - 0.30 = Mild exotherm (e.g., esterification) - 0.50 = Moderate (e.g., hydrogenation) - 1.00 = Severe (e.g., nitration, polymerization) 2. **B. Endothermic Chemical Reactions** (penalty 0 to 0.40) - 0.20 = Calcination, dehydration - 0.40 = Pyrolysis, electrolysis 3. **C. Material Handling and Transfer** (penalty 0 to 1.05) - Based on inventory, transfer method, loading/unloading risk 4. **D. Enclosed or Indoor Process Units** (penalty 0 to 0.90) - 0.45 = Dust collectors, bag filters - 0.90 = Fully enclosed process buildings without adequate ventilation 5. **E. Access** (penalty 0 to 0.35) - Restricted emergency access due to terrain, congestion 6. **F. Drainage and Spill Control** (penalty 0 to 0.50) - 0 = Adequate drainage - 0.50 = No spill containment, sloped toward equipment $$\text{GPH Penalty Total} = \sum_{i=1}^{6} GPH_i$$ ### Step 4: Evaluate Special Process Hazards (SPH) **Special Process Hazards (SPH)** are twelve factors specific to particular process conditions, materials, or quantities. They contribute penalties of **0 to ~8 points** each and represent the *most important* contributors to high F&EI scores. The twelve SPH factors are: 1. **A. Toxic Materials** (penalty 0 to 0.80) 2. **B. Sub-Atmospheric Pressure** (<500 mmHg) (penalty 0 to 0.50) 3. **C. Operation Near or Above Flammable Range** (penalty up to 1.40) - **0.50**: Always in flammable range (storage of flammable liquids) - **1.00**: Occasionally in flammable range - **1.40**: Always near LFL/UFL 4. **D. Dust Explosion Risk** (penalty 0 to 2.00) 5. **E. Relief Pressure** (penalty based on set pressure relative to vessel MAWP) 6. **F. Low Temperature** (penalty 0 to 0.30, for cryogenic brittleness) 7. **G. Quantity of Flammable/Unstable Material** (penalty 0 to 5.00+) — **largest single contributor** 8. **H. Corrosion and Erosion** (penalty 0 to 0.75) 9. **I. Leakage — Joints and Packing** (penalty 0 to 1.50) 10. **J. Use of Fired Equipment** (penalty up to 1.00) 11. **K. Hot Oil Heat Exchange System** (penalty up to 0.85) 12. **L. Rotating Equipment** (penalty 0 to 0.50) $$\text{SPH Penalty Total} = \sum_{i=1}^{12} SPH_i$$ > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Point:** The **Quantity of Flammable Material (SPH-G)** is the single largest contributor to high F&EI scores. As inventory increases from 1,000 lb to 100,000+ lb, the penalty climbs from 0.10 to over 5.0. Students should always check this factor first. ### Step 5: Calculate the Process Unit Hazard Factor (F₃) The Process Unit Hazard Factor combines the GPH and SPH penalties: $$F_1 = \text{GPH Penalty Total}$$ $$F_2 = \text{SPH Penalty Total}$$ $$F_3 = F_1 \times F_2$$ Wait — this is a **common student error**. The correct Dow formula is: $$F_3 = F_1 \times F_2$$ Actually, the correct formulation per Dow's methodology is: $$F_3 = F_1 \times F_2$$ Let me correct this clearly: Per Dow 4th Edition, $F_1$ and $F_2$ are then multiplied: $$\boxed{F_3 = F_1 \times F_2}$$ Where $F_1$ is the sum of GPH penalties and $F_2$ is the sum of SPH penalties. The product yields $F_3$, the Process Unit Hazard Factor. ### Step 6: Calculate the Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI) $$\boxed{F\&EI = F_3 \times MF}$$ ### Step 7: Determine the Hazard Rating and Damage Radius The F&EI value is then classified into one of **five hazard degrees**: | F&EI Range | Hazard Degree | Industry Interpretation | |------------|---------------|--------------------------| | 1 – 60 | **Light** | Normal precautions adequate | | 61 – 96 | **Moderate** | Standard fire protection recommended | | 97 – 127 | **Heavy** | Enhanced protection required | | 128 – 158 | **Severe** | Aggressive loss control mandatory | | ≥ 159 | **Extreme** | Consider process modification; very high risk | The **Radius of Exposure** (in feet) is calculated as: $$R = 0.256 \times F\&EI \text{ (in feet)}$$ The **Area of Exposure** (in ft²) is: $$A = \pi R^2$$ The **Value of the Area at Risk** is the replacement cost of equipment within this radius. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table | # | Parameter | Formula/Definition | Units / Range | Notes | |---|-----------|--------------------|---------------|-------| | 1 | Material Factor (MF) | From NFPA reactivity/flammability rating | 1 to 40 (dimensionless) | Determined first; foundation of rating | | 2 | General Process Hazards (GPH) | $\sum_{i=1}^{6} GPH_i$ | 0 to ~6 (dimensionless) | Six penalty factors | | 3 | Special Process Hazards (SPH) | $\sum_{i=1}^{12} SPH_i$ | 0 to ~30+ (dimensionless) | Twelve penalty factors | | 4 | Process Unit Hazard Factor | $F_3 = GPH \times SPH$ | Dimensionless multiplier | Note: not sum; it is **product** | | 5 | Fire and Explosion Index | $F\&EI = F_3 \times MF$ | Dimensionless | The master index | | 6 | Radius of Exposure | $R = 0.256 \times F\&EI$ | Feet | Convert to meters: $R_m = 0.256 \times 0.3048 \times F\&EI = 0.078 \times F\&EI$ | | 7 | Area of Exposure | $A = \pi R^2$ | ft² (or m²) | Circular damage zone | | 8 | Mond Index (alternative) | $F = B \times \frac{100 + \sum S_i}{100}$ | Dimensionless | ICI methodology | | 9 | Loss Control Credit Factor (LCCF) | $LCCF = \prod C_i$ | 0 to 1 | Multiplies baseline MPPD | | 10 | Maximum Probable Property Damage (MPPD) | $MPPD_{actual} = MPPD_{baseline} \times LCCF$ | Currency units (₹, $) | Final financial exposure | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility In the process industries, the Dow F&EI is applied in: - **Pre-design Safety Reviews** during Front-End Engineering Design (FEED) - **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability) Studies** as a prioritization tool - **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** as a screening layer before detailed consequence modeling - **Insurance Surveys** by IRDA-registered industrial insurers in India - **EPC (Engineering, Procurement, Construction)** contractor selection The Mond Index is preferred in European plants (UK, Germany) and is more elaborate, with 11 hazard factors and 5 inventory penalties. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Numerical Worked Example — KTU Board Style > [!IMPORTANT] > **Worked Example:** A process unit in a petrochemical plant handles **acetone** as the primary material. The process operates at near-atmospheric pressure with mild heating. The unit contains 50,000 lb of acetone.
