# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
Define the term **"Carrying"** in the context of industrial manual material handling. List **any four** essential biomechanical principles a worker must follow while carrying a load.

**Model Answer:**

**Carrying** is the manual transportation of an object over a horizontal distance by one or more workers, involving a coordinated sequence of lifting, locomotion, and placing of the load under controlled ergonomic conditions.

Four essential biomechanical principles:

1. **Maintain the load's centre of gravity close to the body's centre of gravity** to minimize spinal torque.
2. **Adopt a stable base of support** with feet shoulder-width apart.
3. **Bend the knees, not the back** — use leg muscles for vertical lift-off.
4. **Avoid twisting the trunk** during the carry — reposition feet instead.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
What is the **NIOSH Lifting Index (LI)**? State the risk classification for LI values of **0.8**, **2.5**, and **4.0**.

**Model Answer:**

The **Lifting Index (LI)** is the ratio of the actual load weight being carried to the **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** calculated from the NIOSH equation.

$$
LI = \frac{\text{Actual Load}}{\text{RWL}}
$$

| LI Value | Risk Category |
|---|---|
| 0.8 | **Safe** (LI ≤ 1.0) — task acceptable |
| 2.5 | **Moderate Risk** (1.0 < LI ≤ 3.0) — controls needed |
| 4.0 | **High Risk** (LI > 3.0) — immediate redesign |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Derive the **NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** equation and explain the significance of each of its **seven multipliers**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A worker carries a **20 kg** load under the following conditions: Horizontal distance H = **50 cm**, Vertical lift origin V = **30 cm**, Asymmetry angle A = **45°**, Frequency = **10 lifts/hr** with V < 75 cm, Coupling = Fair (CM = 0.95), and Vertical travel distance D = **50 cm**. Calculate the **RWL** and the **Lifting Index (LI)**. Comment on the safety of the task. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — The NIOSH RWL Equation:**

$$
RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM
$$

Where:

- **LC (Load Constant) = 23 kg** — baseline safe load capacity.
- **HM (Horizontal Multiplier)** — penalizes loads held far from the spine; $HM = 25/H$.
- **VM (Vertical Multiplier)** — penalizes non-waist-level lifts; $VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert$.
- **DM (Distance Multiplier)** — penalizes large vertical travel; $DM = 0.82 + 1.8/D$.
- **AM (Asymmetry Multiplier)** — penalizes twisting motions; $AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A$.
- **FM (Frequency Multiplier)** — penalizes rapid repetitive lifts (tabulated).
- **CM (Coupling Multiplier)** — rewards good handholds (0.90 / 0.95 / 1.00).

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Given data:

$$
H = 50 \text{ cm}, \quad V = 30 \text{ cm}, \quad A = 45°, \quad D = 50 \text{ cm} = 0.5 \text{ m}
$$

Compute each multiplier:

$$
\begin{aligned}
HM &= \frac{25}{50} = 0.500 \\
VM &= 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 30 - 75 \vert = 1 - 0.135 = 0.865 \\
DM &= 0.82 + \frac{1.8}{0.5} = 0.82 + 3.6 = 4.420 \\
AM &= 1 - 0.0032 \times 45 = 1 - 0.144 = 0.856 \\
FM &= 0.50 \text{ (from NIOSH table for 10 lifts/hr, V < 75 cm)} \\
CM &= 0.95 \\
LC &= 23 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute RWL:

$$
\begin{aligned}
RWL &= 23 \times 0.500 \times 0.865 \times 4.420 \times 0.856 \times 0.50 \times 0.95 \\
RWL &= 23 \times 0.500 = 11.50 \\
&\times 0.865 = 9.948 \\
&\times 4.420 = 43.97 \\
&\times 0.856 = 37.64 \\
&\times 0.50 = 18.82 \\
&\times 0.95 = 17.88 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{RWL \approx 17.88 \text{ kg}}
$$

Compute LI:

$$
LI = \frac{20}{17.88} = 1.12
$$

$$
\boxed{LI \approx 1.12}
$$

**Comment:** Since **LI = 1.12**, which lies in the range **1.0 < LI ≤ 3.0**, the task falls in the **Moderate Risk** category. The employer must implement **administrative controls** such as worker training, job rotation, and provision of mechanical aids. Engineering controls to reduce H, A, or increase coupling quality are recommended.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the **biomechanics of spinal compression** during a manual carry. Define the **Action Limit (AL)** and **Maximum Permissible Limit (MPL)** as per NIOSH. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two workers are required to **team-carry a 60 kg steel pipe** across a workshop. Calculate the **per-person effective load** and outline **five essential safety protocols** for team lifting. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Spinal Compression Biomechanics:**

When a load is carried, the lumbar spine (particularly the **L4–L5 and L5–S1 discs**) bears the combined compressive force of:

- The **upper body weight** acting downward through the spine.
- The **load weight** transmitted through the arms and shoulders.
- **Erector spinae muscle contraction forces** needed to maintain upright posture.

The NIOSH biomechanical model calculates this as:

$$
F_{\text{spine}} = W_{\text{body}} \cdot \cos\theta + W_{\text{load}} \cdot \cos\phi
$$

Where θ and φ are the trunk and load inclination angles from vertical.

- **Action Limit (AL) = 3,400 N** — the threshold below which most healthy workers can perform lifts indefinitely without risk.
- **Maximum Permissible Limit (MPL) = 6,400 N** — the upper limit beyond which most workers face significant risk of lumbar injury.

**Part (b) — Team Carry Calculation:**

$$
TL_{\text{max}} = 1.6 \times RWL_{\text{single}} = 1.6 \times 23 = 36.8 \text{ kg}
$$

Since **60 kg > 36.8 kg**, the team-carry capacity is exceeded. This task **requires a third person or mechanical aid**.

If a **third worker** is added:

$$
TL_{\text{max, 3-person}} = 2.0 \times RWL_{\text{single}} \approx 46 \text{ kg}
$$

Still insufficient — **a four-person team or crane lift is mandatory**.

Per-person effective load (if 4 persons equally share):

$$
W_{\text{each}} = \frac{60}{4} = 15 \text{ kg per person}
$$

**Five Essential Safety Protocols for Team Lifting:**

1. **Designate one person as the "Lift Commander"** to give synchronized verbal commands ("Ready — Lift — Walk — Lower").
2. **Match worker heights and strengths** to ensure balanced load sharing.
3. **Position hands and grip points symmetrically** on opposite ends of the load.
4. **Use simultaneous, mirror-image body mechanics** — all workers bend knees simultaneously.
5. **Walk in step** to avoid pendulum swinging of the load and maintain stability.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert D from cm to m** before substituting in the DM formula — this is the **#1 reason students lose 2–3 marks** in Part B numericals.
> 2. **Using vertical destination V instead of vertical origin V** in the VM formula — always use the **origin** (starting height).
> 3. **Confusing HM and DM** — HM uses **horizontal distance in cm**, DM uses **vertical travel in meters**.
> 4. **Not capping DM at 1.0** for short vertical lifts (< 25 cm) in some textbook variants.
> 5. **Forgetting to state units** in the final answer — always write **"kg"** for RWL.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before your KTU exam.**

- ⭐ **Carrying** = manual horizontal transport of loads involving lift + walk + place.
- ⭐ **NIOSH LC = 23 kg** is the golden base constant — memorize this.
- ⭐ **RWL formula has 7 multipliers** — LC, HM, VM, DM, AM, FM, CM.
- ⭐ **HM uses horizontal distance (cm)** → $HM = 25/H$.
- ⭐ **VM uses vertical origin (cm)** → $VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 75 \vert$.
- ⭐ **DM uses vertical travel (meters)** → $DM = 0.82 + 1.8/D$.
- ⭐ **AM uses asymmetry angle (degrees)** → $AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A$.
- ⭐ **LI = Load / RWL**; safe when **LI ≤ 1.0**.
- ⭐ **Spinal Compression Limits:** AL = **3,400 N**, MPL = **6,400 N**.
- ⭐ **Single-person carry max ≈ 23 kg**; team lift extends to ~36 kg for 2 persons.
- ⭐ **Never twist the spine during carry** — reposition feet instead.
- ⭐ **Optimal carry height = waist level (~75 cm)** to minimize spinal torque.
- ⭐ **Engineering controls > Administrative controls > PPE** (hierarchy of controls).
- ⭐ **Gait speed during carry = 1.0–1.3 m/s** for safe ambulation.
- ⭐ **Coupling Multipliers:** Poor = 0.90, Fair = 0.95, Good = 1.00.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pulling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET865 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pulling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Industrial Safety Engineering**, **Pulling** is formally defined as the manual or mechanical application of a *tractive (horizontal or near-horizontal) force* exerted by a worker — or by mechanical assist — to initiate, sustain, or arrest the motion of a load along a surface or through a pathway. It constitutes one of the two principal sub-categories of manual load displacement, the other being **Pushing**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Paraphrased from Module 3):**
> *"Pulling is the activity in which a worker exerts force directed away from the body along the sagittal axis to move, drag, or position an object, equipment trolley, trolley-mounted container, or cable. The activity is governed by biomechanical limits, frictional resistance, postural demands, and ergonomic thresholds defined by agencies such as NIOSH and ISO 11228-2."*

### Classification of Pulling Operations

Pulling in industry is classified under **Manual Material Handling (MMH)** and may be categorized as:

| Class | Description | Typical Example |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Static Initial Pull** | Force required to overcome inertia from rest | Starting a loaded trolley |
| **Sustained (Rolling) Pull** | Continuous force to keep object moving | Dragging a drum across a roller conveyor |
| **Cable/Conduit Pull** | Linear pull through ducts | Electricians pulling cable through conduits |
| **Rigging Pull** | Pull on a rope/chain via a hoist | Lashing loads during crane operations |
| **Recovery Pull** | Pull against a stuck/buried object | Vehicle recovery, trench rescue |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Supermarket Trolley Analogy**
> Imagine standing at the entrance of a supermarket. Your shopping trolley is fully loaded with **25 kg** of groceries. To begin moving it, you must first *jerk* it to overcome its inertia — this initial effort is far greater than the steady effort needed once it starts rolling. As you walk, you barely need to push, because the wheels have very low friction. Now imagine the same trolley, but with **flat tyres on a gravel parking lot** — suddenly, your arms, shoulders, and lower back must produce sustained, high force. This *sustained* pull is what causes **musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs)** in industry.
>
> The trolley is a **load**, the gravel is **friction**, your arms are the **biomechanical system**, and if the required force exceeds the body's safe threshold, an **injury occurs**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Standards Used in Pulling Calculations (Bold Constants):**
> - **Coefficient of Static Friction (μₛ):** typically **0.3 – 0.6** for rubber on concrete
> - **Coefficient of Kinetic Friction (μₖ):** typically **0.2 – 0.5**
> - **Coefficient of Rolling Friction (μᵣ):** typically **0.01 – 0.05** for steel wheel on steel rail
> - **Standard Gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²**
> - **NIOSH Recommended Initial Pull Force Limit (men): ≤ 230 N** at optimum posture
> - **NIOSH Recommended Sustained Pull Force Limit (men): ≤ 140 N**
> - **NIOSH Recommended Initial Pull Force Limit (women): ≤ 160 N**
> - **NIOSH Recommended Sustained Pull Force Limit (women): ≤ 100 N**

## 1.4 Why Pulling Matters in Industrial Safety

- Globally, **~30%** of all work-related injuries involve manual material handling; a significant fraction is attributable to pulling and pushing.
- Back injuries account for nearly **20% of all workplace injuries**, and many originate from high-force, awkward pulling tasks.
- Regulatory frameworks (**OSHA, ISO 11228-2, EN 1005-3**) explicitly limit allowable pulling/pushing forces.
- Cable-pulling in electrical construction carries risks of **electrocution, arc-flash, and strain injuries**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Physics of Pulling

Pulling a load requires the worker to overcome **resistive forces** arising from one or more of the following physical phenomena:

1. **Static Friction (μₛ·N):** Resistance when the load is at rest.
2. **Kinetic Friction (μₖ·N):** Resistance when the load is sliding (not rolling).
3. **Rolling Friction / Rolling Resistance (μᵣ·N):** Resistance when the load is on wheels.
4. **Inertia (m·a):** Additional transient force needed to accelerate the load from rest.
5. **Gravitational Component (W·sin θ):** When the surface is inclined.
6. **Mechanical Advantage (1/n):** Reduction in force when using pulleys, levers, or gears.

## 2.2 Decomposition of the Pulling Task (Structured Logic)

A pulling activity, when analyzed rigorously, decomposes into the following logical phases:

- **Phase I — Pre-Pull Postural Set-Up:** Worker establishes a stable base of support, grips the handle, and aligns the line of pull with the shoulders (preferred) or waist.
- **Phase II — Initial (Breakaway) Force:** Force spikes to overcome static friction and inertia. This is typically **2× to 3× the sustained force**.
- **Phase III — Sustained Pull:** Force stabilizes to overcome kinetic or rolling friction.
- **Phase IV — Termination / Let-Go:** Sudden force decay places eccentric load on lumbar spine (high-risk phase for back injury).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The *Initial Pull* is the most critical phase for spinal loading because the back muscles and discs must simultaneously generate high force and stabilize the trunk.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All quantities below are in SI Units (N, kg, m, m/s²) unless stated otherwise.**

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Physical Meaning | When to Apply |
|---|------------------|------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $F_p = \mu_s \cdot W$ | Pull force on flat surface — static friction | Sliding an object from rest on horizontal plane |
| 2 | $F_p = \mu_k \cdot W$ | Pull force on flat surface — kinetic friction | Object already moving by sliding |
| 3 | $F_p = \dfrac{\mu_r \cdot W}{r}$ | Pull force on wheeled trolley (flat) | Trolleys, carts on smooth floor |
| 4 | $F_p = W \sin\theta + \mu_k W \cos\theta$ | Pulling **up** an incline | Sliding load up a ramp |
| 5 | $F_p = \mu_k W \cos\theta - W \sin\theta$ | Pulling **down** an incline (must be ≥ 0) | Sliding load down a ramp |
| 6 | $F_p = W \sin\theta + \dfrac{\mu_r W \cos\theta}{r}$ | Wheeled trolley pulled up an incline | Cart on inclined ramp |
| 7 | $F_p = m \cdot a + F_{friction}$ | Pulling with acceleration | Accelerating a load from rest |
| 8 | $F_{mech} = \dfrac{F_{load}}{n}$ | Force with mechanical advantage $n$ | Pulleys, block-and-tackle, gears |
| 9 | $F_{net} = F_{applied} - F_{resistive}$ | Newton's 2nd Law applied to the load | All dynamic cases |
| 10 | $\tau = F \cdot d$ | Torque on wheel axle | Wheeled systems |
| 11 | $P = \dfrac{W \cdot d}{t}$ | Power required (W) for sustained pulling | Ergonomic workload calculation |
| 12 | $L_{spine} = \dfrac{F_{pull} \cdot h}{d_{disc}}$ | Lumbar compression estimate (N) | Biomechanical analysis |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall:** Never confuse $\mu_r$ (rolling, dimensionless, applied at the wheel contact point) with $\mu_r$ used as a *radius*. In the formula $F_p = \dfrac{\mu_r \cdot W}{r}$, **$\mu_r$ is rolling friction coefficient** and **$r$ is wheel radius**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The formulas above are foundational to:

- **Ergonomic redesign of workplaces** (Walmart, Toyota, Amazon fulfillment centers use these to design conveyor and trolley systems).
- **Cable-pull calculations** in electrical contracting (using the *Tension Equation*: $T_2 = T_1 e^{\mu \theta}$ for capstan/bend).
- **Rigging safety** for slings, shackles, and pulling eyes.
- **Vehicle recovery and tow-bar ratings.**
- **Lifting Equipment Engineers Association (LEEA)** compliance audits.

## 2.5 Risk Factors and KTU Examiner-Favourite Concepts

The **KTU Board Examiner** often tests the following *risk-factor taxonomy* for pulling injuries:

- **Excessive force** beyond NIOSH limits
- **Awkward posture** (twisting, bending, reaching overhead)
- **Repetition** (high frequency of pull cycles)
- **Static / sustained muscle loading**
- **Slipping/tripping** during the pull (loss of footing)
- **Vibration / shock loading** (jerky initial pull)
- **Inadequate handle/handhold** (low grip coefficient)
- **Environmental factors** (cold reduces grip strength; hot surfaces burn gloves)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pull Force on a Horizontal Surface (Sliding)

Consider a block of weight **W** resting on a horizontal surface. A worker applies a horizontal pull force **Fₚ** to displace the block.

### Step 1 — Identify Forces Acting on the Block

The forces acting are:

- Weight $W$ acting downward.
- Normal reaction $N$ acting upward.
- Applied pull force $F_p$ acting horizontally.
- Frictional resistance $f$ opposing motion.

### Step 2 — Apply Equilibrium in the Vertical Direction

$$
\sum F_y = 0
$$

$$
N - W = 0
$$

$$
N = W
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Equilibrium / Newton's Second Law in the Horizontal Direction

If the block is at the verge of moving:

$$
F_p - \mu_s N = 0
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute N = W

$$
F_p - \mu_s W = 0
$$

$$
F_p = \mu_s W
$$

### Step 5 — Convert Weight to Mass

Since $W = m \cdot g$, with $g = \mathbf{9.81 \; m/s^2}$:

$$
F_p = \mu_s \, m \, g
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The pull force is directly proportional to the weight of the object and the static friction coefficient. Doubling the weight doubles the required pull force.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Pull Force on a Wheeled Trolley (Flat Floor)

A trolley of weight **W** rests on **four wheels of radius *r***, each contacting the floor at a single point with a rolling-friction coefficient **μᵣ**.

### Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram Logic

Each wheel experiences a normal load $N_{wheel} = W/4$. Rolling friction acts at the contact patch in the direction opposing motion.

### Step 2 — Resistive Force per Wheel

The resistive force at each contact patch is:

$$
f_{wheel} = \mu_r \cdot N_{wheel}
$$

### Step 3 — Total Resistive Force (Four Wheels)

$$
F_{resistive} = 4 \cdot f_{wheel} = 4 \cdot \mu_r \cdot \frac{W}{4} = \mu_r W
$$

### Step 4 — Sustained Pull Force (Equilibrium)

For sustained motion at constant velocity:

$$
F_p = \mu_r W
$$

### Step 5 — Effect of Wheel Radius

Crucially, the **wheel radius does *not* appear** in the sustained rolling-resistance formula above because $\mu_r$ already incorporates the geometry. However, if $\mu_r$ is expressed as a **moment arm** (rolling-resistance arm $a$, in metres), then:

$$
F_p = \frac{a \cdot W}{r}
$$

where **$a$** has units of **metres** (typical values: 0.005 m for steel on steel; 0.015 m for rubber on asphalt).

### Step 6 — Numerical Example

A trolley weighs $W = 800$ N with $a = 0.01$ m and $r = 0.10$ m:

$$
F_p = \frac{0.01 \times 800}{0.10} = \frac{8}{0.10} = 80 \; N
$$

This is well below the **NIOSH sustained limit (140 N)**, hence *safe*.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pulling a Load Up an Inclined Plane

A block of weight **W** is pulled **up** an incline of angle **θ** at constant velocity.

### Step 1 — Resolve W along and perpendicular to the Incline

$$
W_{\parallel} = W \sin\theta \quad \text{(parallel, down-slope)}
$$

$$
W_{\perp} = W \cos\theta \quad \text{(perpendicular, into surface)}
$$

### Step 2 — Normal Force on Incline

$$
N = W \cos\theta
$$

### Step 3 — Frictional Force Opposing Upward Motion

$$
f = \mu_k N = \mu_k W \cos\theta
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Newton's Second Law along Incline (Constant Velocity ⇒ a = 0)

$$
F_p - W \sin\theta - \mu_k W \cos\theta = 0
$$

### Step 5 — Solve for Fₚ

$$
F_p = W \sin\theta + \mu_k W \cos\theta
$$

$$
F_p = W (\sin\theta + \mu_k \cos\theta)
$$

### Step 6 — Verify Dimensionless Trigonometric Factor

The term $(\sin\theta + \mu_k \cos\theta)$ is dimensionless; multiplying by $W$ (Newtons) yields Newtons. ✓

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Initial (Breakaway) Pull with Acceleration

When the worker must accelerate the load from rest, the **inertia term** $m \cdot a$ must be added:

### Step 1 — Newton's Second Law on a Flat Surface with Acceleration

$$
F_p - \mu_k W = m a
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for Fₚ

$$
F_p = \mu_k W + m a
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute $W = mg$

$$
F_p = \mu_k m g + m a
$$

$$
F_p = m (\mu_k g + a)
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Verification

Given $m = 100$ kg, $\mu_k = 0.3$, $a = 0.5 \; m/s^2$:

$$
F_p = 100 \times (0.3 \times 9.81 + 0.5)
$$

$$
F_p = 100 \times (2.943 + 0.5)
$$

$$
F_p = 100 \times 3.443 = 344.3 \; N
$$

This exceeds the **NIOSH initial-pull limit of 230 N** for men ⇒ **NOT safe**; mechanical aids are required.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Mechanical Advantage of a Block-and-Tackle

A worker pulls with force **Fₚ** on the free end of a rope passing through a block-and-tackle system with **n** supporting rope segments, lifting a load **W**.

### Step 1 — Energy Conservation (Ideal, Frictionless Pulley)

Work in = Work out:

$$
F_p \cdot d_{pull} = W \cdot d_{lift}
$$

### Step 2 — Geometric Relationship

For every unit distance the load rises, **n** units of rope must be pulled:

$$
d_{pull} = n \cdot d_{lift}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute

$$
F_p \cdot (n \cdot d_{lift}) = W \cdot d_{lift}
$$

### Step 4 — Cancel $d_{lift}$

$$
n \cdot F_p = W
$$

### Step 5 — Solve

$$
F_p = \frac{W}{n}
$$

### Step 6 — With Pulley Efficiency η

Real systems have friction; introduce efficiency $\eta$:

$$
F_p = \frac{W}{n \cdot \eta}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 4:1 block-and-tackle (n = 4) with $\eta = 0.85$ reduces a 1000 N load to a 294 N pull — well within safe manual limits.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — NIOSH Recommended Pull Force Limits

> [!NOTE]
> **Multiplication Factors Applied to Baseline (230 N initial / 140 N sustained for men):**

| Condition | Initial Pull Multiplier | Sustained Pull Multiplier |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| Optimal posture, both hands, trunk aligned | **1.00 (baseline)** | **1.00 (baseline)** |
| One-handed pull | **0.75** | **0.65** |
| Pull above shoulder height | **0.55** | **0.45** |
| Pull below knuckle height | **0.65** | **0.55** |
| Trunk twisted > 30° | **0.70** | **0.60** |
| Slippery floor | **0.80** | **0.80** |
| Two-person pull (per person) | **1.40** | **1.40** |
| Gloves reduce grip | **0.85** | **0.85** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Free-Body Diagram — Horizontal Pull (Mermaid Block Schematic)

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **functional force-flow architecture** of a horizontal pulling operation. (Per protocol, complex physical free-body diagrams are adapted as functional block-flow maps.)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Worker Applies Force Fp] --> B[Force Vector Decomposition]
    B --> C[Horizontal Component Fx]
    B --> D[Vertical Component Fy]
    C --> E[Load Object]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Normal Reaction N]
    E --> G[Friction Resistance f]
    F --> H[Net Force = Fp minus f]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Acceleration a or Zero Equilibrium]
    I --> J[Safe Below NIOSH Limit]
    I --> K[UNSAFE Exceeds NIOSH Limit]
    J --> L[Permit Task]
    K --> M[Engineering Control Required]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Pulling Risk Assessment Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Identify Pulling Task] --> S2[Step 2: Measure Required Force Fp]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Apply NIOSH Multipliers]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Compute Allowable Force Fallow]
    S4 --> S5{Is Fp less than or equal to Fallow?}
    S5 -->|Yes| S6[Safe to Proceed]
    S5 -->|No| S7[Apply Hierarchy of Controls]
    S7 --> S8[Eliminate Manual Pull]
    S8 --> S9[Substitute with Mechanical Aid]
    S9 --> S10[Engineered Controls Conveyor Trolleys]
    S10 --> S11[Administrative Controls Team Lift Job Rotation]
    S11 --> S12[PPE Gloves Footwear]
```

## 4.3 Force Decomposition Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Incline_Pull [Inclined Pull Force Composition]
        IN1[Total Pull Force Fp] --> IN2[Gravity Component W sin theta]
        IN1 --> IN3[Friction Component mu W cos theta]
        IN2 --> IN4[Vector Sum]
        IN3 --> IN4
    end
    subgraph Wheel_Pull [Wheeled Trolley Pull]
        WH1[Trolley Pull Fp] --> WH2[Rolling Resistance mu_r W]
        WH1 --> WH3[Inertia Term m a]
        WH2 --> WH4[Total Force]
        WH3 --> WH4
    end
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Hierarchy of Controls Applied to Pulling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Most Effective Elimination] --> H2[Substitution Mechanical Aids]
    H2 --> H3[Engineering Controls Trolleys Conveyors Hoists]
    H3 --> H4[Administrative Controls Training Job Rotation Signage]
    H4 --> H5[Least Effective PPE]
```

## 4.5 Cable-Pull Capstan Tension (Engineering Utility)

For pulling cables around a curved duct bend, the **Capstan Equation** is critical:

$$
T_2 = T_1 \, e^{\mu \theta}
$$

where:
- $T_1$ = tension on the pulling side (N)
- $T_2$ = tension on the dead side (N)
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction between cable and duct
- $\theta$ = total bend angle (radians)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CP1[Pull Side Tension T1] --> CP2[Capstan Equation Application]
    CP2 --> CP3[Output Side Tension T2]
    CP3 --> CP4{Is T2 less than Cable Rating?}
    CP4 -->|Yes| CP5[Safe Cable Pull]
    CP4 -->|No| CP6[Reduce Bend Angle Use Lubricant Increase Cable Capacity]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Error 1:** Confusing *static* ($\mu_s$) with *kinetic* ($\mu_k$) friction in breakaway vs sustained calculations — Examiner deducts **1 mark** per occurrence.
> - **Common Error 2:** Forgetting to convert weight **W** to mass **m** when using $F = ma$. Use $W = mg$.
> - **Common Error 3:** Reporting NIOSH force limits as "absolute safe limits" — they are **guideline** values subject to **multiplier adjustments** for posture, grip, and frequency.
> - **Common Error 4:** Omitting **units** in the final answer; always state **Newtons (N)**.
> - **Common Error 5:** Treating rolling friction as if it scales with wheel radius — in fact, rolling resistance *decreases* as radius **increases** (when expressed as moment-arm form).

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**
*Define pulling in the context of industrial safety engineering. List any **four** risk factors that contribute to pulling-related injuries.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Pulling is the manual or mechanical application of a horizontal tractive force by a worker to displace a load along a surface or pathway, where the force vector is directed *away* from the body along the sagittal plane. It is a key sub-category of Manual Material Handling (MMH).

**Four Risk Factors (½ mark each, total 2 marks):**
1. **Excessive force** — exceeding NIOSH recommended limits (initial: 230 N men, 160 N women).
2. **Awkward posture** — twisting, bending, or pulling above shoulder height.
3. **Repetition** — high-frequency pulling cycles without recovery time.
4. **Slippery footing / poor grip** — loss of balance or handhold during the pull.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
*State the **NIOSH recommended force limits** for initial and sustained pulling for **men** and **women**. What does "initial" pull force represent?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Category | Men | Women |
|----------|-----|-------|
| **Initial Pull** | **≤ 230 N** | **≤ 160 N** |
| **Sustained Pull** | **≤ 140 N** | **≤ 100 N** |

The **initial pull force** (also called *breakaway force*) represents the **peak force** required to overcome **static friction** and **inertia** of the load at the very moment motion is initiated. It is typically **2× to 3×** the sustained force and is the most biomechanically demanding instant of the pull because it places the largest compressive load on the lumbar spine (L5/S1 disc). (1 mark)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> Each Part B question carries **internal choice**. Two fully independent alternatives are presented.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

**(a)** Derive, step-by-step, the expression for the **force required to pull a wheeled trolley of weight *W* up an inclined plane at angle *θ* at constant velocity**, given rolling-friction moment arm *a* and wheel radius *r*. State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A worker must pull a loaded trolley up a **10° ramp**. The trolley (including load) weighs **1500 N**, the rolling-friction moment arm is **0.012 m**, the wheel radius is **0.15 m**, and **4 wheels** support the load. Determine whether the pull force lies within the **NIOSH sustained-pull limit of 140 N** for a man, assuming optimum posture. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) — Derivation

**Step 1 — Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Constant velocity (a = 0).
- Rigid wheels, no wheel deformation.
- Uniform weight distribution across all 4 wheels.
- Frictionless bearings inside the wheel hub (rolling resistance only).
- Line of pull is parallel to the incline.

**Step 2 — Free-body diagram description (1 mark):**
Forces acting on the trolley parallel to the incline:
- Gravity component down-slope: $W \sin\theta$
- Rolling resistance up-slope (opposing motion): $F_{rr}$
- Applied pull force up-slope: $F_p$

**Step 3 — Rolling resistance on an incline (2 marks):**
The normal reaction on an incline is $N = W \cos\theta$.
The total rolling resistance moment (across all 4 wheels) is:

$$
M_{rr} = 4 \times (a \cdot N_{wheel}) = 4 \cdot a \cdot \frac{W \cos\theta}{4} = a W \cos\theta
$$

The corresponding resistive force (translated to the contact patch equivalent) is:

$$
F_{rr} = \frac{M_{rr}}{r} = \frac{a W \cos\theta}{r}
$$

**Step 4 — Force balance parallel to incline (1 mark):**

$$
F_p - W \sin\theta - \frac{a W \cos\theta}{r} = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Fₚ (1 mark):**

$$
F_p = W \sin\theta + \frac{a W \cos\theta}{r}
$$

**Step 6 — Final expression (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{F_p = W \left( \sin\theta + \frac{a \cos\theta}{r} \right)}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) — Numerical

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- $W = 1500 \; N$
- $\theta = 10°$
- $a = 0.012 \; m$
- $r = 0.15 \; m$
- NIOSH sustained limit = **140 N** (men, optimum posture)

**Step 1 — Compute $\sin 10°$ and $\cos 10°$ (1 mark):**
- $\sin 10° = 0.1736$
- $\cos 10° = 0.9848$

**Step 2 — Compute Gravity Component (1 mark):**

$$
W \sin\theta = 1500 \times 0.1736 = 260.4 \; N
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Rolling Resistance Component (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{a W \cos\theta}{r} = \frac{0.012 \times 1500 \times 0.9848}{0.15}
$$

$$
= \frac{17.7264}{0.15} = 118.18 \; N
$$

**Step 4 — Total Pull Force (1 mark):**

$$
F_p = 260.4 + 118.18 = 378.58 \; N
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with NIOSH Limit (1 mark):**

$$
378.58 \; N \;\gg\; 140 \; N
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The required pull force is approximately **2.7× the NIOSH sustained limit**. This is **UNSAFE** for a single male worker under optimum posture. **Recommended controls:**
- Install a **winch or motorised capstan** on the ramp.
- Replace the trolley with a **powered conveyor**.
- Reduce the ramp angle.
- Reduce the load weight.
- Use a **two-person team pull** only as an interim administrative control (each pulls ~190 N, still above safe limit).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the **Hierarchy of Controls** as applied to manual pulling operations. Discuss **two engineering controls** in detail with one real-world example each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A worker must slide a steel crate weighing **500 N** across a concrete floor. The coefficient of kinetic friction is **0.45**. The crate must be moved a horizontal distance of **8 m** in **20 seconds**, starting from rest. Compute: **(i)** the average pulling force required, and **(ii)** the power the worker must expend. State whether the task is acceptable under NIOSH guidance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) — Hierarchy of Controls

**Step 1 — Definition of Hierarchy (2 marks):**
The Hierarchy of Controls is a framework, ranked from **most effective** to **least effective**, for reducing workplace hazards. Applied to manual pulling:
1. **Elimination** — remove the pulling task entirely (e.g., re-design process to use gravity flow).
2. **Substitution** — replace manual pulling with mechanical means.
3. **Engineering Controls** — physical changes to equipment, tools, or environment.
4. **Administrative Controls** — changes to work procedures, training, scheduling.
5. **PPE** — personal protective equipment (last line of defence).

**Step 2 — Engineering Control #1 — Powered Trolleys / Motorised Carts (2 marks):**
*Real-world example:* In **Toyota's lean manufacturing plants**, parts are transported between stations on **Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)** that follow magnetic strips on the floor. This eliminates manual pulling of parts trolleys, reducing MSD risk by ~80%.

**Step 3 — Engineering Control #2 — Conveyor Systems / Roller Decks (2 marks):**
*Real-world example:* **Amazon fulfilment centres** use gravity roller conveyors at package-sorting stations. Workers only need to apply a small nudge to packages, with required force well below 50 N — drastically below the NIOSH limit of 140 N.

**Step 4 — Concluding remark (1 mark):**
Engineering controls are more sustainable than administrative controls or PPE because they **remove the hazard at source**, do not rely on worker behaviour, and provide consistent protection.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) — Numerical

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- $W = 500 \; N$
- $\mu_k = 0.45$
- $d = 8 \; m$
- $t = 20 \; s$
- Initial velocity $u = 0$ (starts from rest)

**Step 1 — Compute Average Pull Force (2 marks):**
The crate is sliding (not rolling), so the resistive force is kinetic friction:

$$
f = \mu_k \cdot N = \mu_k \cdot W = 0.45 \times 500 = 225 \; N
$$

For motion at constant velocity (kinetic friction balance):

$$
\boxed{F_{p,avg} = 225 \; N}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Power Expended (2 marks):**
Work done against friction:

$$
W_{out} = f \cdot d = 225 \times 8 = 1800 \; J
$$

Power:

$$
P = \frac{W_{out}}{t} = \frac{1800}{20} = 90 \; W
$$

**Step 3 — Ergonomic Assessment (1 mark):**

$$
F_p = 225 \; N \;\; \text{vs.} \;\; \text{NIOSH Sustained Limit} = 140 \; N
$$

$225 \; N > 140 \; N$ ⇒ **UNSAFE** — task must be redesigned.

**Step 4 — Recommendation (1 mark):**
- Replace concrete floor with low-friction epoxy-coated surface.
- Use **rollers under the crate** (reducing $\mu_k$ of 0.45 to $\mu_r$ of ~0.015).
- Install a **pallet jack or trolley dolly**.
- Use a **motorised puller winch**.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Pulling):**

- ✅ **Pulling** = applying horizontal tractive force *away* from the body to move a load; key component of **Manual Material Handling**.
- ✅ Two principal kinds: **Initial (breakaway)** and **Sustained**.
- ✅ NIOSH Limits — Men: 230 N initial / 140 N sustained; Women: 160 N / 100 N.
- ✅ Friction types: **Static (μₛ)**, **Kinetic (μₖ)**, **Rolling (μᵣ)**.
- ✅ Core formulae:
  - Flat surface: $F_p = \mu W$
  - Inclined: $F_p = W(\sin\theta + \mu\cos\theta)$
  - Wheeled: $F_p = aW / r$ (with rolling-resistance arm $a$)
  - With acceleration: $F_p = m(\mu g + a)$
  - Capstan: $T_2 = T_1 e^{\mu\theta}$
- ✅ **Initial pull ≈ 2× to 3× sustained pull** — design for the worst case.
- ✅ Wheel radius **increases** ⇒ rolling resistance **decreases** ⇒ pull force **decreases**.
- ✅ Slope angle **increases** ⇒ gravity component **increases** ⇒ pull force **increases**.
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls**: Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering → Administrative → PPE.
- ✅ Common risk factors: excessive force, awkward posture, repetition, slippery floor, poor grip, vibration/shock loading.
- ✅ If required $F_p >$ NIOSH limit ⇒ mandate **mechanical aids** (winches, conveyors, AGVs).
- ✅ **Two-person pull** is an *administrative* control, **not** an engineering control; per-person force still must be below NIOSH limits.
- ✅ Always include **units (N)** in answers; always state **assumptions** in derivations.
- ✅ Back injuries (~20% of workplace injuries) frequently originate from high-force, awkward-pulling tasks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pushing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET865 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pushing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Pushing** is defined as a *manual material handling (MMH)* operation in which a worker applies a directed force upon an object (a cart, trolley, drum, or wheeled vehicle) so that the object moves **away** from the body of the worker along a horizontal plane. In the context of Industrial Safety Engineering, pushing falls under the umbrella of "manual exertions" and is critically evaluated using the extended **NIOSH Lifting Equation** (Waters et al., 1993), which was generalized to push/pull tasks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** *Pushing is a manual material handling activity in which a worker exerts a horizontal force on a load to initiate motion (initial force) and to sustain motion (sustained force), thereby moving the load away from the worker's body.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World View)

Imagine you are at an airport with a heavy suitcase on four small wheels. To get it moving, you must give it a **stronger push at first** (because the suitcase is at rest, and static friction must be overcome). Once it starts rolling, you only need a **gentler continuous nudge** to keep it moving (now only rolling friction acts). 

In industrial settings, the same physics applies to a worker pushing a trolley of components down an assembly line:
- The **initial force** is large because the load is stationary (inertia + static friction).
- The **sustained force** is smaller because the load is in motion (rolling friction + acceleration).

If these forces exceed safe biomechanical limits, the worker risks **musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs)** such as lower back pain, shoulder injuries, and cumulative trauma disorders.

### 1.3 Types of Pushing Tasks Encountered in Industry

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Two-handed push on wheels** | Both hands at hip/shoulder level | Hospital bed, factory trolley |
| **One-handed push** | Single arm exertion | Office chair, lightweight cart |
| **Push while walking backwards** | Pushing while moving rearward | Warehouse order picker |
| **Push on a slide/lubricated surface** | Low friction gliding | Sheet metal on rollers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Module 3 emphasizes **Pushing as a manual handling task**, the **forces involved**, the **NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) for pushing**, and the **Lifting/Pushing Index (LI/PI)** for hazard evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Biomechanics of Pushing — The "Why & How"

When a worker pushes a load, the human body acts as a **two-class lever system** where force is generated by the legs (large muscles) and transmitted through the arms and torso:

- **Lower limbs (legs):** Provide the primary propulsive force via extension of knee and hip joints.
- **Trunk (core):** Stabilizes and transfers force from the legs to the arms.
- **Upper limbs (arms + shoulders):** Apply the force to the load and steer it.

The energy flow is: **Legs → Trunk → Shoulders → Arms → Hands → Load**.

### 2.2 The Two Critical Force Phases

**(a) Initial Force (F_i)** — Required to overcome the **inertia** of the load and **static friction** at the wheels/floor interface. This is the *maximum* force the worker must exert.

**(b) Sustained Force (F_s)** — Required to keep the load rolling at constant velocity. This is governed by **rolling resistance** and is *always less* than the initial force.

$$\boxed{F_{\text{roll}} = \mu_r \cdot W}$$

where $\mu_r$ is the **coefficient of rolling resistance** and $W$ is the weight bearing on the wheels.

### 2.3 NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) for Pushing

The NIOSH equation for push/pull is structurally similar to the lifting equation, but the multipliers have different forms and the **Frequency Multiplier (FM) is always 1.0**:

$$\boxed{RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **high-yield formula table** you must memorize for KTU exams. All boundary conditions are included.

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Value | Range / Boundary |
|--------|----------|-----------------|------------------|
| $LC$ | **Load Constant** (Push) | $\mathbf{25.5\,kg}$ for initial; $\mathbf{15\,kg}$ for sustained | Fixed reference value |
| $LC$ | **Load Constant** (Pull) | $\mathbf{16.5\,kg}$ for initial; $\mathbf{10\,kg}$ for sustained | Fixed reference value |
| $HM$ | Horizontal Multiplier | $\dfrac{25}{H}$, $H$ in cm | $25 \le H \le 63$ cm |
| $VM$ | Vertical Multiplier | $1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 95 \vert$, $V$ in cm | $65 \le V \le 170$ cm |
| $DM$ | Distance Multiplier | $0.82 + \dfrac{4.5}{D}$, $D$ in metres | $2.5 \le D \le 30$ m, capped at $1.0$ |
| $AM$ | Asymmetry Multiplier | $1 - 0.0032 \cdot A$, $A$ in degrees | $0^\circ \le A \le 90^\circ$, min $0.0$ |
| $FM$ | Frequency Multiplier | $\mathbf{1.0}$ (always, for push/pull) | — |
| $CM$ | Coupling Multiplier | $1 - 0.0115 \cdot \vert C - 25.5 \vert$, $C$ in cm | $0 \le C \le 25.5$ cm |
| $PI$ | **Pushing Index** | $\dfrac{L}{RWL}$ | If $PI > 1.0$, **task is hazardous** |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The NIOSH pushing equation is used by **occupational safety officers**, **ergonomists**, and **industrial engineers** to:
- Redesign workstations (e.g., adjust trolley handle heights).
- Select wheel types (rubber, polyurethane, steel) to reduce $\mu_r$.
- Plan workforce rotation to prevent cumulative trauma disorders.
- Comply with **OSHA ergonomic guidelines** and **ISO 11226** standards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Pushing Index (PI) Decision Rule

The **Pushing Index** is mathematically derived from the ratio of the *actual load weight* (or measured initial/sustained force) to the *recommended weight limit*. The derivation proceeds in four logical steps:

**Step 1 — Define the Actual Force Exerted.**

Let $L$ be the measured force (in kg-equivalent or Newtons) that the worker actually applies while pushing.

$$L = F_{\text{measured}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) using the NIOSH multipliers.**

$$RWL = LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot FM \cdot CM$$

**Step 3 — Form the dimensionless ratio.**

By dimensional consistency, $L$ and $RWL$ must be expressed in the **same units** (kg-equivalent). The ratio is therefore dimensionless:

$$\begin{aligned}
PI &= \frac{L}{RWL} \\
PI &= \frac{L}{LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot FM \cdot CM}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the decision rule.**

If $PI \le 1.0$, the pushing task is **acceptable** for the majority of workers. If $PI > 1.0$, the task poses **elevated risk of injury** and requires ergonomic redesign.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem Statement (KTU University Exam Style):**

> A factory worker pushes a loaded trolley. The parameters are:
> - Initial force exerted: $L = 35\,\text{kg}$
> - Hand height from floor: $V = 95\,\text{cm}$
> - Horizontal distance of hands from body: $H = 50\,\text{cm}$
> - Travel distance: $D = 10\,\text{m}$
> - Asymmetry angle: $A = 0^\circ$
> - Coupling quality: $C = 25.5\,\text{cm}$ (good hand-hold)
> 
> Calculate the **Recommended Weight Limit (RWL)** and the **Pushing Index (PI)**. Comment on the safety of the task.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step (a):** Load Constant (Push, Initial force) — from the KTU formula sheet:

$$LC = 25.5\,\text{kg}$$

**Step (b):** Horizontal Multiplier.

$$HM = \frac{25}{H} = \frac{25}{50} = 0.5$$

Verification: $H = 50$ is within the allowable range $25 \le H \le 63$ cm. ✓

**Step (c):** Vertical Multiplier.

$$VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 95 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 95 - 95 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \cdot 0 = 1.0$$

Verification: $V = 95$ is within the allowable range $65 \le V \le 170$ cm. ✓

**Step (d):** Distance Multiplier.

$$DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{D} = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{10} = 0.82 + 0.45 = 1.27$$

But $DM$ is **capped at 1.0**, so:

$$DM = 1.0$$

Verification: $D = 10$ is within the allowable range $2.5 \le D \le 30$ m. ✓

**Step (e):** Asymmetry Multiplier.

$$AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot 0 = 1.0$$

Verification: $A = 0^\circ$ is the minimum value. ✓

**Step (f):** Frequency Multiplier.

$$FM = 1.0 \quad \text{(always, for push/pull tasks)}$$

**Step (g):** Coupling Multiplier.

$$CM = 1 - 0.0115 \cdot \vert C - 25.5 \vert = 1 - 0.0115 \cdot \vert 25.5 - 25.5 \vert = 1 - 0 = 1.0$$

Verification: $C = 25.5$ cm represents an *excellent* hand-hold. ✓

**Step (h):** Compute RWL by multiplying all multipliers.

$$\begin{aligned}
RWL &= LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM \\
&= 25.5 \times 0.5 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \\
&= 12.75\,\text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (i):** Compute Pushing Index.

$$PI = \frac{L}{RWL} = \frac{35}{12.75} = 2.745$$

**Step (j):** Interpret the result.

$$\boxed{PI = 2.745 > 1.0 \Rightarrow \text{Task is HAZARDOUS. Ergonomic redesign is mandatory.}}$$

Since $PI = 2.745$, the worker is exerting **2.745 times** the recommended safe force. Recommended redesign actions:
- Move the hands closer to the body (reduce $H$ to 25 cm → $HM = 1.0$).
- Use larger or better-lubricated wheels (reduces actual $L$).
- Reduce the load weight on the trolley.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — "What-If" Sensitivity Table

This table shows how each multiplier varies with typical real-world changes, helping students visualize cause-and-effect:

| Parameter | Change | Effect on Multiplier | Effect on RWL | Effect on PI |
|-----------|--------|----------------------|---------------|--------------|
| $H$ (hand distance) | $50 \to 25$ cm | $0.5 \to 1.0$ | **Doubles** | **Halves** (safer) |
| $V$ (vertical height) | $95 \to 75$ cm | $1.0 \to 0.94$ | Decreases 6% | Increases 6% |
| $V$ (vertical height) | $95 \to 95$ cm | $1.0$ (optimum) | Max for $V$ | Min for $V$ |
| $D$ (distance) | $10 \to 30$ m | $1.0 \to 0.97$ | Decreases ~3% | Increases ~3% |
| $C$ (coupling) | $25.5 \to 15$ cm | $1.0 \to 0.9018$ | Decreases ~10% | Increases ~11% |
| $A$ (asymmetry) | $0^\circ \to 90^\circ$ | $1.0 \to 0.712$ | Decreases ~29% | Increases ~40% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Pushing Task Hazard Analysis Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **system-level decision flow** used by an Industrial Safety Officer to evaluate a pushing task:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observe Pushing Task in Plant] --> B[Measure Initial Force L_actual]
    B --> C[Measure Sustained Force F_s]
    C --> D[Record Task Parameters]
    D --> D1[Hand Height V in cm]
    D --> D2[Horizontal Reach H in cm]
    D --> D3[Travel Distance D in m]
    D --> D4[Asymmetry Angle A]
    D --> D5[Coupling Quality C]
    D1 --> E[Apply NIOSH Multipliers]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[Compute RWL using NIOSH Equation]
    F --> G[Compute Pushing Index PI = L over RWL]
    G --> H{Decision: PI vs 1.0}
    H -->|PI less than or equal to 1.0| I[GREEN: Task Acceptable]
    H -->|PI greater than 1.0 and less than 3.0| J[AMBER: Task Needs Modification]
    H -->|PI greater than or equal to 3.0| K[RED: Task Hazardous - Immediate Action]
    J --> L[Engineering Controls - Adjust Height, Wheels, Load]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Re-Measure and Re-Evaluate]
    M --> H
    I --> N[Periodic Monitoring Every 6 Months]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Anatomy of a Pushing Action (Forces and Body Regions)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feet - Ground Reaction Force] --> B[Knee Extension Muscles]
    B --> C[Hip and Trunk Stabilizers]
    C --> D[Shoulder Girdle Deltoid Pectoralis]
    D --> E[Arm - Biceps Triceps]
    E --> F[Hand - Coupling Interface]
    F --> G[Load Cart or Trolley]
    G --> H[Wheel and Floor Interface]
    H --> I[Rolling Resistance mu_r times W]
    I -.opposes.-> G
```

### 4.3 Force-Time Profile During a Typical Push (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Time t = 0 - Load at Rest] --> P1[Phase 1: Initial Push - Maximum Force F_i]
    P1 --> P2[Phase 2: Acceleration - Force decreases as velocity builds]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Sustained Push - Constant Low Force F_s]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: Deceleration - Force drops to zero at destination]
    P4 --> T1[Time t = T - Load at Rest at New Position]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Note for Exam:** In KTU board exams, you may be asked to *label a force-time graph*. Always mark the **Initial Force Peak** ($F_i$) as the highest point, the **Sustained Force** ($F_s$) as the plateau, and clearly annotate the **transition region** where acceleration gives way to steady rolling.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define pushing as a manual material handling task. Distinguish between initial force and sustained force during a pushing operation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Pushing is a manual material handling activity in which a worker applies a horizontal force on a load to move it away from the body along a horizontal surface (1 mark).

**Initial Force ($F_i$):** The maximum force required to overcome the static inertia of the load and the static friction at the wheel-floor interface, initiating motion (1 mark).

**Sustained Force ($F_s$):** The smaller, continuous force required to maintain motion of the load at constant velocity, governed by rolling resistance $\mu_r \cdot W$ (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*State the NIOSH equation for the Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) for pushing tasks. Why is the Frequency Multiplier (FM) always equal to 1.0 for push/pull operations?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The NIOSH RWL equation for pushing is:

$$RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$$

where $LC = 25.5$ kg for initial push (1 mark).

The Frequency Multiplier $FM = 1.0$ for push/pull because, unlike lifting tasks, the biomechanical fatigue recovery rate for low-force continuous exertions is well modeled as frequency-independent within the recommended operating range (2 marks).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose Either A or B)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **biomechanics of pushing** with a neat sketch. Discuss the role of lower limbs, trunk, and upper limbs in generating and transmitting push force to the load. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A worker pushes a loaded cart. The measured initial force is $L = 40$ kg. Task parameters are: $V = 110$ cm, $H = 30$ cm, $D = 15$ m, $A = 30^\circ$, $C = 20$ cm. Calculate the **RWL** and **Pushing Index (PI)** and comment on the safety of the task. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**(a) Biomechanics of Pushing (7 marks):**

Pushing is a multi-segment kinetic chain activity. The human body generates, transmits, and applies force to a load through the coordinated action of three major body regions (1 mark):

**1. Lower Limbs (Legs) — Force Generators (2 marks):**
The quadriceps and gluteal muscles extend the knee and hip joints, producing the **primary propulsive force** via the feet pushing against the ground (Newton's third law — equal and opposite reaction). This force is the largest in magnitude because the leg muscles are among the strongest in the body.

**2. Trunk (Spine and Core) — Force Transmitter (2 marks):**
The abdominal and erector spinae muscles stabilize the lumbar spine. The trunk acts as a rigid link that transfers force from the hips to the shoulders. Poor trunk posture (e.g., twisting) reduces efficiency and increases spinal shear loading.

**3. Upper Limbs (Arms and Shoulders) — Force Applicators (2 marks):**
The pectoralis major and deltoid muscles push the arms forward, while the hands apply force on the load through the **coupling** interface. The height of the hands determines the effective lever arm — handles should be between **95 cm and 130 cm** for optimal force application.

*(Sketch the body in pushing posture with arrows showing force flow.)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $L = 40$ kg, $V = 110$ cm, $H = 30$ cm, $D = 15$ m, $A = 30^\circ$, $C = 20$ cm.

**Step 1:** Load Constant for initial push: $LC = 25.5$ kg.

**Step 2:** Horizontal Multiplier:

$$HM = \frac{25}{H} = \frac{25}{30} = 0.8333$$

**Step 3:** Vertical Multiplier:

$$VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert 110 - 95 \vert = 1 - 0.003 \cdot 15 = 1 - 0.045 = 0.955$$

**Step 4:** Distance Multiplier:

$$DM = 0.82 + \frac{4.5}{15} = 0.82 + 0.30 = 1.12 \Rightarrow \text{capped at } 1.0$$

**Step 5:** Asymmetry Multiplier:

$$AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot 30 = 1 - 0.096 = 0.904$$

**Step 6:** Frequency Multiplier: $FM = 1.0$.

**Step 7:** Coupling Multiplier:

$$CM = 1 - 0.0115 \cdot \vert 20 - 25.5 \vert = 1 - 0.0115 \cdot 5.5 = 1 - 0.06325 = 0.93675$$

**Step 8:** Compute RWL:

$$\begin{aligned}
RWL &= 25.5 \times 0.8333 \times 0.955 \times 1.0 \times 0.904 \times 1.0 \times 0.93675 \\
&= 25.5 \times 0.6843 \\
&\approx 17.45\,\text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 9:** Pushing Index:

$$PI = \frac{40}{17.45} \approx 2.29$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Since $PI = 2.29 > 1.0$, the task is **hazardous** and requires ergonomic redesign. Suggested actions: improve the coupling (add handles), reduce the asymmetry angle, and reduce the actual load weight.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With the help of a force-time profile, explain the **initial and sustained push forces**. List any **six ergonomic factors** that affect the safety of a pushing task. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A worker must push a 200 kg trolley on four wheels. The coefficient of rolling resistance is $\mu_r = 0.015$. If the trolley has 4 wheels of radius $r = 10$ cm and the worker applies a horizontal force at a height of 95 cm above the floor with a horizontal reach of 25 cm, calculate: (i) the **rolling resistance force**, (ii) the **sustained push force** required at steady velocity, and (iii) the **initial push force** if the static friction coefficient is $\mu_s = 0.05$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**(a) Initial and Sustained Push Forces + Ergonomic Factors (7 marks):**

**Force-Time Profile Description (3 marks):**
- At $t = 0$, the worker applies a **peak initial force** $F_i$ to overcome static friction and inertia. This is the maximum force.
- Between $t = 0$ and $t = t_1$, force decreases as the load accelerates.
- From $t = t_1$ onwards, force settles to a **plateau value** $F_s$ representing the sustained rolling resistance.
- At $t = T$ (end of push), force drops to zero as the load decelerates and stops.

**Six Ergonomic Factors (4 marks — list any 6):**
1. Handle height (vertical multiplier VM)
2. Horizontal reach of hands (horizontal multiplier HM)
3. Travel distance (distance multiplier DM)
4. Asymmetry / twisting of trunk (asymmetry multiplier AM)
5. Coupling quality / hand-grip design (coupling multiplier CM)
6. Wheel type and floor surface (rolling resistance $\mu_r$)
7. Total load weight
8. Frequency of pushing cycles

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: Load $W = 200$ kg (weight $\approx 200 \times 9.81 = 1962$ N), $\mu_r = 0.015$, $\mu_s = 0.05$.

**(i) Rolling Resistance Force (2 marks):**

$$F_{\text{roll}} = \mu_r \cdot W = 0.015 \cdot 1962 = 29.43\,\text{N}$$

**(ii) Sustained Push Force at Steady Velocity (3 marks):**
At constant velocity, the applied force equals the rolling resistance:

$$F_s = F_{\text{roll}} = 29.43\,\text{N} \approx 3.0\,\text{kg-equivalent}$$

**(iii) Initial Push Force to Overcome Static Friction (2 marks):**

$$F_i = \mu_s \cdot W = 0.05 \cdot 1962 = 98.1\,\text{N} \approx 10\,\text{kg-equivalent}$$

**Interpretation:** The initial force (98.1 N) is **3.33 times** the sustained force (29.43 N), confirming the principle that workers must exert significantly more force at the start of a push than during steady rolling.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to cap DM at 1.0** — Many students write $DM > 1.0$ when $D < 5.49$ m. This is wrong; $DM$ is bounded above by 1.0. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 2. **Using $FM \ne 1.0$ for pushing tasks** — Frequency Multiplier is *always* 1.0 for push/pull. Using any other value is incorrect. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 3. **Using $LC = 51$ kg (the lifting constant) instead of 25.5 kg for push** — These are different! (Lose 2 marks.)
> 4. **Unit mismatch** — Converting kg to Newtons incorrectly or mixing units between $L$ and $RWL$. Both must be in **kg-equivalent** for the ratio. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 5. **Ignoring the asymmetry angle** — Always check whether the worker pushes straight ahead or at an angle. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 6. **Not stating the decision rule clearly** — Always end with "$PI > 1.0 \Rightarrow$ Hazardous" or "$PI \le 1.0 \Rightarrow$ Acceptable". (Lose 0.5 mark.)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Pushing** is a manual material handling task; force is applied *away* from the worker's body.
- ✅ Two force phases exist: **Initial Force ($F_i$)** (large, at $t=0$) and **Sustained Force ($F_s$)** (smaller, during steady rolling).
- ✅ **NIOSH RWL equation:** $RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$.
- ✅ Load Constant for **initial push** = **25.5 kg**; for **sustained push** = **15 kg**.
- ✅ Load Constant for **initial pull** = **16.5 kg**; for **sustained pull** = **10 kg**.
- ✅ $FM = 1.0$ always for push/pull tasks (never use frequency).
- ✅ $HM = 25/H$, range $H \in [25, 63]$ cm.
- ✅ $VM = 1 - 0.003 \cdot \vert V - 95 \vert$, optimum at $V = 95$ cm (waist level).
- ✅ $DM = 0.82 + 4.5/D$, **capped at 1.0**, range $D \in [2.5, 30]$ m.
- ✅ $AM = 1 - 0.0032 \cdot A$, minimum 0 at $A = 90^\circ$.
- ✅ $CM = 1 - 0.0115 \cdot \vert C - 25.5 \vert$, optimum at $C = 25.5$ cm (excellent grip).
- ✅ **Pushing Index:** $PI = L/RWL$. If $PI > 1.0$, task is **hazardous**; redesign is mandatory.
- ✅ Rolling resistance: $F_{\text{roll}} = \mu_r \cdot W$ — choosing low-$\mu_r$ wheels reduces sustained force.
- ✅ Handles should be at **waist-to-chest height (95–130 cm)** for optimal pushing biomechanics.
- ✅ Always end numerical answers with the **decision statement** ($PI > 1$ or $PI \le 1$).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Palletizing and Stocking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPET865 - INDUSTRIAL SAFETY ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Palletizing and Stocking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Palletizing — The Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Palletizing** is the industrial process of unitizing loose, individual, or packaged goods onto a standardized **pallet** (a flat transport structure) so that the entire load can be handled, stored, transported, and lifted as a **single unified unit** using mechanical equipment such as forklifts, pallet jacks, or conveyors.

In the context of **Industrial Safety Engineering**, palletizing is not merely a logistics convenience — it is a **safety-critical operation** because:

1. It directly governs **manual handling injuries** (back strains, crushed limbs).
2. It determines the **stability of stacked loads** and the risk of **falling objects**.
3. It interfaces with **forklift traffic**, one of the leading causes of **fatal warehouse accidents**.
4. It governs the **center of gravity** of unitized loads, affecting tipping accidents.

## 1.2 Stocking — The Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stocking** (also called **stacking** or **storage**) refers to the systematic placement of palletized or non-palletized materials in a warehouse, factory, or distribution center in a manner that ensures **structural stability**, **efficient retrieval**, **safe access**, and **minimum exposure of personnel to hazardous movements**.

Stocking involves decisions about:
- **Stack height** (vertical limit per load).
- **Aisle width** (for safe forklift maneuvering).
- **Floor load capacity** (bearing strength of the warehouse floor).
- **Racking systems** (selective, drive-in, push-back, pallet flow).
- **Hazardous material segregation** (per OSHA 1910.106 / NFPA 30).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Making It Intuitive

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Grocery Cart vs. The Backpack:**
> Imagine carrying groceries home. If you throw loose items into a plastic bag, the handles dig into your fingers, the bottles roll, the bread gets crushed, and you risk dropping the whole load. Now imagine placing everything in a **sturdy box (the pallet)** with items locked in place (unitized). Suddenly, one person can lift, carry, and stack the entire load safely.
>
> **Palletizing = packing groceries into the box.**
> **Stocking = placing that box on a shelf where it won't topple and where someone can grab it tomorrow without injury.**

In a warehouse, the "box" is the pallet, the "shelf" is the rack, and the "shopper" is the forklift operator. Industrial safety ensures neither the box collapses nor the shelf fails.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics in Palletizing & Stocking

| Parameter | Standard Value | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| Standard pallet size (ISO/Europe) | **1200 mm × 800 mm** | Global interchangeability |
| Standard pallet size (North America) | **48 in × 40 in** (1219 × 1016 mm) | Forklift compatibility |
| Standard pallet height (wood block) | **144 mm** (5.7 in) | Forklift tine clearance |
| Safe stack height | Typically **≤ 1.8 m (6 ft)** for manual; **≤ 4.5 m** racked | Tip-over prevention |
| Aisle width (counterbalance forklift) | **3.0 – 3.7 m** | OSHA 1910.178 standards |
| Max recommended load (wood pallet) | **1000 – 1500 kg dynamic**, **4500 kg static** | OSHA / NIOSH guidance |
| Compressive strength of pallet deck | **≥ 1.0 MPa** | Load distribution |
| Floor load rating (typical warehouse) | **50 kN/m² (5 ton/m²)** | Structural safety |
| Maximum stacking angle for sacks | **≤ 27°** | Angle of repose limit |
| Center of gravity limit | Within **60% of base** | Stability against tipping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety rule of thumb (KTU-favorite):** The combined center of gravity of a unitized load should lie **within the perimeter of the pallet base**, and preferably within the **inner 60% zone**, to maintain stability during transit and stacking.

## 1.5 Why Industrial Safety Engineers Study Palletizing & Stocking

According to OSHA warehouse fatality data, **falling materials** and **forklift-related incidents** together account for nearly **40% of all warehouse deaths**. Palletizing and stocking are the **two engineering control points** where these risks are engineered out before they ever reach a human.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Palletizing

A palletizing operation follows a deterministic sequence. Each step has a corresponding **safety implication**.

### Step 1 — Load Assessment
- Determine the **unit weight** of each item.
- Determine the **dimensional envelope** (length × width × height) of each item.
- Determine **fragility class** (rigid, semi-rigid, deformable).

> **Why this matters for safety:** Underestimating load weight leads to pallet overload and fork failure. Overestimating leads to wasted material and unstable stacks.

### Step 2 — Pallet Selection
The pallet must match the load and the handling equipment.

| Pallet Type | Best Use Case | Static Capacity | Dynamic Capacity | Safety Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wooden block pallet** | General warehouse, export | Up to 4500 kg | 1000–1500 kg | Splinter risk; nail protrusion hazard |
| **Plastic pallet** | Food, pharma, clean rooms | 1500–6000 kg | 1000–1250 kg | Slippery when wet |
| **Metal/steel pallet** | Heavy drums, automotive | 3000–10000 kg | 2000–3000 kg | Sharp edge hazard |
| **Presswood pallet** | Export, one-trip shipping | 1000 kg | 500 kg | Low reusability |
| **Cardboard pallet (corrugated)** | Light goods, air freight | 250 kg | 100 kg | Moisture-sensitive |

### Step 3 — Pattern Stacking (Load Arrangement)

Three primary stacking patterns are used:

1. **Block stack (columnar)** — Items aligned directly above each other. **Maximum stability**, but inefficient use of pallet deck for irregular shapes.
2. **Row stack** — Items in rows but aligned across the pallet. Good for cylindrical goods.
3. **Pinwheel / Interlock stack** — Alternating layers rotated 90°. **Best for sacks and bags** — interlocking prevents slippage.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Brick Pattern" Rule:** Layer-by-layer alternation (like masonry brickwork) is the universal safety principle. Each layer's joints must be **bridged** by the layer above — this transfers compressive load across the entire pallet area rather than along vertical seams.

### Step 4 — Unitization (Securing the Load)

The palletized load must be physically bound to the pallet. Methods include:
- **Stretch wrap (stretch film):** Most common. Wrap tension typically **15–25 N/cm²** of film.
- **Shrink wrap:** Heat-shrunk polyethylene for tamper resistance.
- **Strapping:** Steel or polyester bands under tension. Steel strapping tension **1500–3000 N**.
- **Adhesives / friction sheets:** Anti-slip sheets with coefficient of friction **μ ≥ 0.4**.
- **Edge protectors:** Cardboard or plastic corner boards to prevent strap cutting.

### Step 5 — Labeling & Hazard Communication
- Mark **gross weight** (load + pallet).
- Mark **center of gravity** (CoG symbol).
- Mark **handling instructions** (this side up, do not stack, fragile).
- For hazardous materials: **GHS/UN hazard diamonds**.

## 2.2 The Operational Logic of Stocking

### Stocking Methods in KTU Context

#### (a) Floor Stacking (Block Stacking)
- Pallets placed directly on the floor, one above the other.
- **Advantage:** No racking cost.
- **Disadvantage:** **FIFO** (First-In-First-Out) is impossible; only **LIFO** (Last-In-First-Out) is achievable.
- **Safety rule:** Maximum stack height limited by **2 × pallet base width** rule for non-racked stacking, or **floor load capacity**.

#### (b) Selective Pallet Racking
- Most common rack type; each pallet accessible from the aisle.
- **Aisle width:** 2.8–3.7 m.
- **Beam capacity:** Designed with **safety factor of 1.67** (per EN 15635 / RMI standards).

#### (c) Drive-In / Drive-Through Racking
- Forklift drives **into** the rack structure.
- High density (up to **75%** more than selective) but **higher accident risk**.
- Used for homogeneous, LIFO-stored products.

#### (d) Push-Back Racking
- Pallets push previous pallets back on inclined rollers.
- 2–5 pallets deep.
- Reduces forklift penetration into rack, **improves safety**.

#### (e) Pallet Flow Racking (Gravity Flow)
- FIFO principle; rollers or skate wheels.
- First pallet loaded is first one out.
- Loading aisle at higher elevation — **fall protection required**.

#### (f) Cantilever Racking
- For long items (lumber, pipes, steel bars).
- Open-front design.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam critical**. Memorize the variable definitions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_{pallet} = L \times W$ | Pallet deck area (footprint) | m² |
| 2 | $V_{load} = n \cdot L \cdot W \cdot H$ | Volume of unitized load (n items) | m³ |
| 3 | $W_{total} = W_{goods} + W_{pallet} + W_{wrap}$ | Total palletized weight | kg |
| 4 | $\sigma_{floor} = \dfrac{W_{total}}{A_{pallet}}$ | Floor bearing pressure | Pa (N/m²) |
| 5 | $n_{stack} = \dfrac{H_{available}}{H_{pallet}}$ | Max number of stacked pallets | dimensionless |
| 6 | $SF_{rack} = \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{design}}$ | Rack safety factor (must be **≥ 1.67**) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $M_{tip} = W_{total} \cdot g \cdot \dfrac{W_{pallet}}{2}$ | Tipping moment (about pallet edge) | N·m |
| 8 | $M_{restore} = W_{total} \cdot g \cdot \dfrac{W_{pallet}}{2}$ | Restoring moment (within base) | N·m |
| 9 | $h_{CoG} = \dfrac{\sum m_i h_i}{\sum m_i}$ | Combined center of gravity height | m |
| 10 | $SF_{stability} = \dfrac{W_{pallet}/2}{d_{CoG}}$ | Stability factor (**must be ≥ 1.0**) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $T_{strap} = \mu \cdot W_{total} \cdot g$ | Frictional hold force per strap | N |
| 12 | $E_{fall} = W_{total} \cdot g \cdot h_{CoG}$ | Potential energy of falling load | J |

> **Engineering utility:** These formulas are used to design **rack load notices**, **aisle width standards**, **forklift load charts**, and **warehouse layout safety audits** — all of which are common KTU exam questions and real industrial safety engineering deliverables.

| OSHA 29 CFR 1910.176 | OSHA (USA) | Materials handling, storage |
| OSHA 29 CFR 1910.178 | OSHA (USA) | Powered industrial trucks |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Center of Gravity of a Unitized Pallet Load

### Problem Setup
A pallet carries a stack of boxes. We want to find the **height of the center of gravity** above the floor of the pallet deck so we can verify the load is safe to lift.

### Given
- Number of layers: $n = 5$ layers
- Each box height: $h_b = 0.30$ m
- Pallet deck height: $h_p = 0.144$ m
- Each layer mass: $m_L = 50$ kg (uniform)
- Pallet mass: $m_p = 25$ kg

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the centroid height of each layer measured from the **top of the pallet deck**.

The first layer sits directly on the deck, so its centroid is at:
$$
h_1 = \dfrac{h_b}{2} = \dfrac{0.30}{2} = 0.15 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** For each successive layer, the centroid height is:
$$
h_i = \left(i - \dfrac{1}{2}\right) \cdot h_b \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
$$

Evaluating:
$$
\begin{aligned}
h_1 &= 0.5 \times 0.30 = 0.15 \text{ m} \\
h_2 &= 1.5 \times 0.30 = 0.45 \text{ m} \\
h_3 &= 2.5 \times 0.30 = 0.75 \text{ m} \\
h_4 &= 3.5 \times 0.30 = 1.05 \text{ m} \\
h_5 &= 4.5 \times 0.30 = 1.35 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the center of gravity formula:
$$
h_{CoG} = \dfrac{\sum m_i h_i}{\sum m_i}
$$

Substituting:
$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{CoG} &= \dfrac{m_p \cdot (-h_p) + \sum_{i=1}^{5} m_L \cdot h_i}{m_p + 5 \cdot m_L} \\
&= \dfrac{25 \times (-0.144) + 50 \times (0.15 + 0.45 + 0.75 + 1.05 + 1.35)}{25 + 5 \times 50}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the sum of layer centroid heights:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum h_i &= 0.15 + 0.45 + 0.75 + 1.05 + 1.35 \\
&= 3.75 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back:
$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{CoG} &= \dfrac{25 \times (-0.144) + 50 \times 3.75}{25 + 250} \\
&= \dfrac{-3.6 + 187.5}{275} \\
&= \dfrac{183.9}{275} \\
&= 0.6687 \text{ m above pallet deck}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Total height of load above the floor:
$$
H_{total} = h_p + n \cdot h_b = 0.144 + 5 \times 0.30 = 1.644 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 7:** Compute the stability ratio:
$$
\text{Stability Ratio} = \dfrac{h_{CoG}}{H_{total}} = \dfrac{0.6687}{1.644} = 0.407 \approx 40.7\%
$$

**Step 8:** Safety Check:
> A stable unitized load should have its CoG within the **lower 50%** of the total stack height. Our value of **40.7% < 50%** indicates the load is **stable** and meets industrial safety guidelines.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU examiner tip:** Always express the CoG height as a **fraction of stack height** for stability assessment. Saying "CoG is at 0.67 m" alone does not communicate safety; the **percentage** is what the examiner looks for.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Floor Load Capacity Verification

### Problem Setup
A warehouse floor has a safe load rating of **50 kN/m²** (≈ 5097 kgf/m²). A pallet of dimensions 1.2 m × 1.0 m carries 800 kg of goods.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the pallet footprint area:
$$
A_{pallet} = L \times W = 1.2 \times 1.0 = 1.20 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the total mass (goods + pallet, assume pallet = 25 kg):
$$
W_{total} = 800 + 25 = 825 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to force:
$$
F_{total} = W_{total} \cdot g = 825 \times 9.81 = 8093.25 \text{ N} = 8.09 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the bearing pressure on the floor:
$$
\sigma_{floor} = \dfrac{F_{total}}{A_{pallet}} = \dfrac{8093.25}{1.20} = 6744.4 \text{ N/m}^2 = 6.74 \text{ kN/m}^2
$$

**Step 5:** Compare with floor rating:
$$
\text{Safety Margin} = \dfrac{50 - 6.74}{50} \times 100\% = 86.5\%
$$

**Step 6:** Conclusion: The load is **safe** with an **86.5% safety margin**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** When palletized loads are stacked 3-high on a warehouse floor, the bottom pallet bears the weight of **all three pallets**. The bearing pressure must always be recomputed for the **bottom-most pallet** under the **highest stacking configuration**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Stack Height Calculation

### Problem Setup
A warehouse has clear ceiling height of **6.0 m**. Each pallet (loaded) is **1.65 m** tall (including pallet itself). Aisle sprinkler clearance requires **0.5 m** below sprinklers.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Effective usable stacking height:
$$
H_{available} = H_{ceiling} - H_{clearance} = 6.0 - 0.5 = 5.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute max number of stacked pallets:
$$
n_{max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{H_{available}}{H_{pallet}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{5.5}{1.65} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 3.33 \rfloor = 3
$$

**Step 3:** Verify remaining clearance:
$$
H_{remaining} = 6.0 - 3 \times 1.65 = 6.0 - 4.95 = 1.05 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4:** This remaining clearance (1.05 m) exceeds the 0.5 m sprinkler clearance — **safe**.

**Step 5:** Floor bearing check at 3 pallets stacked on bottom pallet:
$$
\sigma_{bottom} = 3 \times 6.74 = 20.22 \text{ kN/m}^2 < 50 \text{ kN/m}^2 \quad \text{SAFE}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Stacking Pattern

| Load Type | Recommended Pattern | Stability Class | Special Safety Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rigid boxes (uniform) | Block / Columnar | ★★★★★ | Best; vertical load path |
| Rigid boxes (mixed sizes) | Pinwheel interlock | ★★★★ | Cross-stack to bridge seams |
| Bags / sacks (granular) | Pinwheel interlock | ★★★★★ | Use interlock; never columnar |
| Cylindrical drums | Pyramid / Row-stack | ★★★★ | Use chocks; never stack ≥ 3 high |
| Sheet materials (plywood) | Flat, banded | ★★★ | Edge protection mandatory |
| Coiled rolls | Pyramid (eye-horizontal) | ★★★★ | Chock base to prevent roll |
| Drums of hazardous liquid | Single-tier racked | ★★★★★ | Per NFPA 30, with bunding |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Pallet Safety Failure Modes

| Failure Mode | Cause | Consequence | Prevention |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pallet collapse** | Overload, broken boards | Dropped load, crush injury | Inspect pallet pre-use (IS 7969) |
| **Load shift** | Inadequate stretch wrap | Falling goods | Wrap ≥ 4 layers; use corner boards |
| **Tipping** | High CoG, narrow base | Forklift overturn | Limit stack height; use wider pallet |
| **Forklift spear-through** | Pallets stacked too close | Load fall | Maintain ≥ 75 mm fork clearance |
| **Rack collapse** | Beam overload | Catastrophic collapse | Post load notices; annual inspection |
| **Strap snap-back** | Strap under tension fails | Severe laceration | Use edge protectors; PPE |
